[[sn:Zaō Gongen]] [[en:Zaō Gongen]] [[de:Za%C5%8D Gongen]] [[zh:%E8%97%8F%E7%8E%8B%E6%AC%8A%E7%8F%BE]] [[cs:Za%C3%B3 gongen]] [[ja:蔵王権現]] [[sn:aō Gongen]]

[[File:蔵王権現立像-Zaō Gongen MET DT5160.jpg|thumb|銅造蔵王権現立像 [[メトロポリタン美術館]]蔵 平安時代（11世紀）<ref>所蔵先館のクレジットラインは以下のとおり。The Harry G. C. Packard Collection of Asian Art, Gift of Harry G. C. Packard, and Purchase, Fletcher, Rogers, Harris Brisbane Dick, and Louis V. Bell Funds, Joseph Pulitzer Bequest, and The Annenberg Fund Inc. Gift, 1975</ref>]] [[File:Zaou Gongen Circumstancial appearance of Mount Yoshino.jpg|thumb|蔵王権現（[[ギメ東洋美術館]]）]] [[File:ŌmineSanjō 2020a.jpg|thumb|湧出岩（[[山上ヶ岳]]）]]
'''Zaō Gongen''' (蔵王権現) is the principal deity of {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a form of Japanese mountain ascetic Buddhism. The formal name is '''Kongō Zaō Gongen''' (金剛蔵王権現) or '''Kongō Zaō Bosatsu''' (金剛蔵王菩薩)<ref>‘Konjaku Monogatari-shū,’ the legend of En no Gyōja.</ref>. This deity is uniquely Japanese, with no origins in Indian Buddhism, and is enshrined as the principal deity of {{ill|Kinpusen-ji|ja|金峯山寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Yoshino|ja|吉野町|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The name "Kongō Zaō" signifies the ultimate, indestructible truth and the ruler governing all things<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction’ in ‘The Deity of Shugendō: Zaō Gongen,’ p.10–17.</ref>. The term {{ill|Gongen|ja|権現|qq=draft|qq=draft}} means “a deity manifesting temporarily in a provisional form.” Zaō Gongen is believed to encompass the powers of all {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Bodhisattvas|ja|菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, deities, and spirits<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.18–21, p.26–28.</ref>.
== Origins == Zaō Gongen is said to have manifested to {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref>A mountain ascetic from the 7th century.</ref> during his training at {{ill|Mount Kinpu|ja|金峰山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Yoshino<ref>Rinetaka Tanaka, ‘Introduction to Shugendō,’ Shueisha Shinsho, 2014, p.54.</ref>. It is considered a composite of {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|千手観音|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Maitreya|ja|弥勒菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. At {{ill|Kinpusen-ji|ja|金峯山寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on Mount Yoshino, three nearly identical statues of Zaō Gongen are enshrined together as the temple’s principal icons.
Under the {{ill|Shinbutsu Shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} system, Zaō Gongen was identified with {{ill|Emperor Ankan|ja|安閑天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Hirokuni Oshitaka Kanehi-no-Mikoto), and during the {{ill|Meiji-era|ja|明治時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|qq=draft}} policy, many shrines that previously worshipped Zaō Gongen reidentified their deities as Emperor Ankan.
In {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Zaō Gongen became syncretized with deities such as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kunitokotachi|ja|国之常立神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kanayamahiko|ja|金山彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. As a result, shrines enshrining Zaō Gongen often list these deities as their enshrined gods.
Zaō Gongen was also equated with {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|スサノオ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the medieval period, particularly linked through the {{ill|Gakuen-ji|ja|鰐淵寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} temple.
== Iconography == === Appearance === Zaō Gongen is depicted similarly to {{ill|Vidyaraja|ja|明王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with a fierce, wrathful expression and wild, upward-flowing hair. He usually holds a {{ill|vajra|ja|三鈷杵|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in his right hand and forms a sword mudra with his left. His right leg is raised high, symbolizing the crushing of evil, while his left foot stands firmly on the ground. Flames blaze behind him, representing wisdom. Some statues, like those at {{ill|Kōryū-ji|ja|広隆寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Kyoto, show both feet grounded, while others have variations with reversed postures.
One notable example is the main deity at {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}’s {{ill|Nageire-dō|ja|投入堂|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a designated Important Cultural Property from the Heian period.
=== Symbolism === The vajra represents the destruction of delusions, while the sword mudra symbolizes cutting through desires. His left foot suppresses demons, and the raised right foot disperses evil between heaven and earth. The flames signify enlightenment<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.22–25.</ref>.
== Zaō Mountain == {{ill|Mount Zaō|ja|蔵王山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} spans {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Originally known as {{ill|Katta-mine|ja|刈田嶺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or {{ill|Mount Fubo|ja|不忘山|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it was later named after Zaō Gongen when the deity was enshrined during the Heian period.
In the Edo period, it became a popular pilgrimage site. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|1=Haibutsu kishaku|qq=draft|qq=draft}} movement, shrines like {{ill|Katta Mine Shrine|ja|刈田嶺神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} redefined their enshrined deities, but the worship of Zaō Gongen persisted.
== Major Zaō Gongen Sites == * {{ill|Kinpusen-ji|ja|金峯山寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Nara Prefecture) – Important Cultural Property * {{ill|Nyoi-in Temple|ja|如意輪寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Nara Prefecture) – Statue by the Kei school (1226 CE) * {{ill|Ishizuchi-san|ja|石鎚山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} temples (Ehime Prefecture) * {{ill|Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|ja|大井蔵王権現神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Tokyo)
== Mantras and Seed Syllables == * **Standard Mantra:** "On Bakiryū Sowaka" (オン・バキリュウ・ソワカ)<ref name="mantra">‘The Book of Mudras and Mantras,’ Gakken, 2004, p.152.</ref> * **Other Mantras:** "On Basakusha Aranja Sowaka," "On Basara Kusha Aranja Un Sowaka" * **Seed Syllable:** "हूं" (Hūṃ)<ref name="mantra"/>
== Zaō Gongen in Culture == * {{ill|Arashiyama (Noh)|ja|嵐山 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – Appears in Noh theatre * {{ill|Kusu (Noh)|ja|国栖 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – Featured in Noh performance * {{ill|Hanagasa Ondo|ja|花笠音頭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – Mentioned in folk songs == See Also == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

== Zaō Gongen in Culture == * {{ill|Arashiyama (Noh)|ja|嵐山 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – Appears in Noh theatre * {{ill|Kusu (Noh)|ja|国栖 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – Featured in Noh performance * {{ill|Hanagasa Ondo|ja|花笠音頭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – Mentioned in folk songs == See Also == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

[[sn:Zaō Gongen]] [[en:Zaō Gongen]] [[de:Za%C5%8D Gongen]] [[zh:%E8%97%8F%E7%8E%8B%E6%AC%8A%E7%8F%BE]] [[cs:Za%C3%B3 gongen]] [[ja:蔵王権現]] [[sn:aō Gongen]]

revision.text:
[[File:蔵王権現立像-Zaō Gongen MET DT5160.jpg|thumb|銅造蔵王権現立像 [[メトロポリタン美術館]]蔵 平安時代（11世紀）<ref>所蔵先館のクレジットラインは以下のとおり。The Harry G. C. Packard Collection of Asian Art, Gift of Harry G. C. Packard, and Purchase, Fletcher, Rogers, Harris Brisbane Dick, and Louis V. Bell Funds, Joseph Pulitzer Bequest, and The Annenberg Fund Inc. Gift, 1975</ref>]] [[File:Zaou Gongen Circumstancial appearance of Mount Yoshino.jpg|thumb|蔵王権現（[[ギメ東洋美術館]]）]] [[File:ŌmineSanjō 2020a.jpg|thumb|湧出岩（[[山上ヶ岳]]）]]
'''Zaō Gongen''' (蔵王権現) is the principal deity of {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō|qq=}}, a form of Japanese mountain ascetic Buddhism. The formal name is '''Kongō Zaō Gongen''' (金剛蔵王権現) or '''Kongō Zaō Bosatsu''' (金剛蔵王菩薩)<ref>‘Konjaku Monogatari-shū,’ the legend of En no Gyōja.</ref>. This deity is uniquely Japanese, with no origins in Indian Buddhism, and is enshrined as the principal deity of {{ill|Kinpusen-ji|ja|金峯山寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpusen-ji|qq=}} in {{ill|Yoshino|ja|吉野町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshino|qq=}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Prefecture|qq=}}. The name "Kongō Zaō" signifies the ultimate, indestructible truth and the ruler governing all things<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction’ in ‘The Deity of Shugendō: Zaō Gongen,’ p.10–17.</ref>. The term {{ill|Gongen|ja|権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongen|qq=}} means “a deity manifesting temporarily in a provisional form.” Zaō Gongen is believed to encompass the powers of all {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhas|qq=}}, {{ill|Bodhisattvas|ja|菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bodhisattvas|qq=}}, deities, and spirits<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.18–21, p.26–28.</ref>.
== Origins == Zaō Gongen is said to have manifested to {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|qq=}}<ref>A mountain ascetic from the 7th century.</ref> during his training at {{ill|Mount Kinpu|ja|金峰山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kinpu|qq=}} in Yoshino<ref>Rinetaka Tanaka, ‘Introduction to Shugendō,’ Shueisha Shinsho, 2014, p.54.</ref>. It is considered a composite of {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|千手観音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon|qq=}}, and {{ill|Maitreya|ja|弥勒菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maitreya|qq=}}. At {{ill|Kinpusen-ji|ja|金峯山寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpusen-ji|qq=}} on Mount Yoshino, three nearly identical statues of Zaō Gongen are enshrined together as the temple’s principal icons.
Under the {{ill|Shinbutsu Shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu Shūgō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=}} system, Zaō Gongen was identified with {{ill|Emperor Ankan|ja|安閑天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ankan|qq=}} (Hirokuni Oshitaka Kanehi-no-Mikoto), and during the {{ill|Meiji-era|ja|明治時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji-era|qq=}} {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu Bunri|qq=}} policy, many shrines that previously worshipped Zaō Gongen reidentified their deities as Emperor Ankan.
In {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}, Zaō Gongen became syncretized with deities such as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=}}, {{ill|Kunitokotachi|ja|国之常立神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitokotachi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kanayamahiko|ja|金山彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanayamahiko|qq=}}. As a result, shrines enshrining Zaō Gongen often list these deities as their enshrined gods.
Zaō Gongen was also equated with {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|スサノオ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} at {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Taisha|qq=}} in the medieval period, particularly linked through the {{ill|Gakuen-ji|ja|鰐淵寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuen-ji|qq=}} temple.
== Iconography == === Appearance === Zaō Gongen is depicted similarly to {{ill|Vidyaraja|ja|明王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vidyaraja|qq=}}, with a fierce, wrathful expression and wild, upward-flowing hair. He usually holds a {{ill|vajra|ja|三鈷杵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/vajra|qq=}} in his right hand and forms a sword mudra with his left. His right leg is raised high, symbolizing the crushing of evil, while his left foot stands firmly on the ground. Flames blaze behind him, representing wisdom. Some statues, like those at {{ill|Kōryū-ji|ja|広隆寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōryū-ji|qq=}} in Kyoto, show both feet grounded, while others have variations with reversed postures.
One notable example is the main deity at {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|qq=}}’s {{ill|Nageire-dō|ja|投入堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nageire-dō|qq=}} in {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Prefecture|qq=}}, a designated Important Cultural Property from the Heian period.
=== Symbolism === The vajra represents the destruction of delusions, while the sword mudra symbolizes cutting through desires. His left foot suppresses demons, and the raised right foot disperses evil between heaven and earth. The flames signify enlightenment<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.22–25.</ref>.
== Zaō Mountain == {{ill|Mount Zaō|ja|蔵王山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Zaō|qq=}} spans {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyagi Prefecture|qq=}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamagata Prefecture|qq=}}. Originally known as {{ill|Katta-mine|ja|刈田嶺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta-mine|qq=}} or {{ill|Mount Fubo|ja|不忘山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fubo|qq=}}, it was later named after Zaō Gongen when the deity was enshrined during the Heian period.
In the Edo period, it became a popular pilgrimage site. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}} and the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|1=Haibutsu kishaku|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haibutsu Kishaku|13=User:Immanuelle/Haibutsu kishaku|qq=}} movement, shrines like {{ill|Katta Mine Shrine|ja|刈田嶺神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta Mine Shrine|qq=}} redefined their enshrined deities, but the worship of Zaō Gongen persisted.
== Major Zaō Gongen Sites == * {{ill|Kinpusen-ji|ja|金峯山寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpusen-ji|qq=}} (Nara Prefecture) – Important Cultural Property * {{ill|Nyoi-in Temple|ja|如意輪寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoi-in Temple|qq=}} (Nara Prefecture) – Statue by the Kei school (1226 CE) * {{ill|Ishizuchi-san|ja|石鎚山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizuchi-san|qq=}} temples (Ehime Prefecture) * {{ill|Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|ja|大井蔵王権現神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|qq=}} (Tokyo)
== Mantras and Seed Syllables == * **Standard Mantra:** "On Bakiryū Sowaka" (オン・バキリュウ・ソワカ)<ref name="mantra">‘The Book of Mudras and Mantras,’ Gakken, 2004, p.152.</ref> * **Other Mantras:** "On Basakusha Aranja Sowaka," "On Basara Kusha Aranja Un Sowaka" * **Seed Syllable:** "हूं" (Hūṃ)<ref name="mantra"/>
== Zaō Gongen in Culture == * {{ill|Arashiyama (Noh)|ja|嵐山 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arashiyama (Noh)|qq=}} – Appears in Noh theatre * {{ill|Kusu (Noh)|ja|国栖 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusu (Noh)|qq=}} – Featured in Noh performance * {{ill|Hanagasa Ondo|ja|花笠音頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanagasa Ondo|qq=}} – Mentioned in folk songs == See Also == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Buddhas|qq=}}
{{Shinto-Buddhism}} {{Medieval Shinto}}

One notable example is the main deity at {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|qq=}}’s {{ill|Nageire-dō|ja|投入堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nageire-dō|qq=}} in {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Prefecture|qq=}}, a designated Important Cultural Property from the Heian period.

His left foot suppresses demons, and the raised right foot disperses evil between heaven and earth. The flames signify enlightenment<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.22–25.</ref>.
== Zaō Mountain == {{ill|Mount Zaō|ja|蔵王山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Zaō|qq=}} spans {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|ja|宮城県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyagi Prefecture|qq=}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamagata Prefecture|qq=}}. Originally known as {{ill|Katta-mine|ja|刈田嶺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta-mine|qq=}} or {{ill|Mount Fubo|ja|不忘山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fubo|qq=}}, it was later named after Zaō Gongen when the deity was enshrined during the Heian period.
In the Edo period, it became a popular pilgrimage site. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}} and the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|1=Haibutsu kishaku|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haibutsu Kishaku|13=User:Immanuelle/Haibutsu kishaku|qq=}} movement, shrines like {{ill|Katta Mine Shrine|ja|刈田嶺神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta Mine Shrine|qq=}} redefined their enshrined deities, but the worship of Zaō Gongen persisted.
== Major Zaō Gongen Sites == * {{ill|Kinpusen-ji|ja|金峯山寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpusen-ji|qq=}} (Nara Prefecture) – Important Cultural Property * {{ill|Nyoi-in Temple|ja|如意輪寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoi-in Temple|qq=}} (Nara Prefecture) – Statue by the Kei school (1226 CE) * {{ill|Ishizuchi-san|ja|石鎚山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizuchi-san|qq=}} temples (Ehime Prefecture) * {{ill|Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|ja|大井蔵王権現神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|qq=}} (Tokyo)
== Mantras and Seed Syllables == * **Standard Mantra:** "On Bakiryū Sowaka" (オン・バキリュウ・ソワカ)<ref name="mantra">‘The Book of Mudras and Mantras,’ Gakken, 2004, p.152.</ref> * **Other Mantras:** "On Basakusha Aranja Sowaka," "On Basara Kusha Aranja Un Sowaka" * **Seed Syllable:** "हूं" (Hūṃ)<ref name="mantra"/>
== Zaō Gongen in Culture == * {{ill|Arashiyama (Noh)|ja|嵐山 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arashiyama (Noh)|qq=}} – Appears in Noh theatre * {{ill|Kusu (Noh)|ja|国栖 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusu (Noh)|qq=}} – Featured in Noh performance * {{ill|Hanagasa Ondo|ja|花笠音頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanagasa Ondo|qq=}} – Mentioned in folk songs == See Also == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Buddhas|qq=}}

[[sn:Zaō Gongen]] [[en:Zaō Gongen]] [[de:Za%C5%8D Gongen]] [[zh:%E8%97%8F%E7%8E%8B%E6%AC%8A%E7%8F%BE]] [[cs:Za%C3%B3 gongen]] [[ja:蔵王権現]] [[sn:aō Gongen]]

revision.text:
[[File:蔵王権現立像-Zaō Gongen MET DT5160.jpg|thumb|銅造蔵王権現立像 [[メトロポリタン美術館]]蔵 平安時代（11世紀）<ref>所蔵先館のクレジットラインは以下のとおり。The Harry G. C. Packard Collection of Asian Art, Gift of Harry G. C. Packard, and Purchase, Fletcher, Rogers, Harris Brisbane Dick, and Louis V. Bell Funds, Joseph Pulitzer Bequest, and The Annenberg Fund Inc. Gift, 1975</ref>]] [[File:Zaou Gongen Circumstancial appearance of Mount Yoshino.jpg|thumb|蔵王権現（[[ギメ東洋美術館]]）]] [[File:ŌmineSanjō 2020a.jpg|thumb|湧出岩（[[山上ヶ岳]]）]]
'''Zaō Gongen''' (蔵王権現) is the principal deity of {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō|qq=}}, a form of Japanese mountain ascetic Buddhism. The formal name is '''Kongō Zaō Gongen''' (金剛蔵王権現) or '''Kongō Zaō Bosatsu''' (金剛蔵王菩薩)<ref>‘Konjaku Monogatari-shū,’ the legend of En no Gyōja.</ref>. This deity is uniquely Japanese, with no origins in Indian Buddhism, and is enshrined as the principal deity of {{ill|Kinpusen-ji|ja|金峯山寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpusen-ji|qq=}} in {{ill|Yoshino|ja|吉野町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshino|qq=}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Prefecture|qq=}}. The name "Kongō Zaō" signifies the ultimate, indestructible truth and the ruler governing all things<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction’ in ‘The Deity of Shugendō: Zaō Gongen,’ p.10–17.</ref>. The term {{ill|Gongen|ja|権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongen|qq=}} means “a deity manifesting temporarily in a provisional form.” Zaō Gongen is believed to encompass the powers of all {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhas|qq=}}, {{ill|Bodhisattvas|ja|菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bodhisattvas|qq=}}, deities, and spirits<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.18–21, p.26–28.</ref>.
== Origins == Zaō Gongen is said to have manifested to {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|qq=}}<ref>A mountain ascetic from the 7th century.</ref> during his training at {{ill|Mount Kinpu|ja|金峰山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kinpu|qq=}} in Yoshino<ref>Rinetaka Tanaka, ‘Introduction to Shugendō,’ Shueisha Shinsho, 2014, p.54.</ref>. It is considered a composite of {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|千手観音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon|qq=}}, and {{ill|Maitreya|ja|弥勒菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maitreya|qq=}}. At {{ill|Kinpusen-ji|ja|金峯山寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpusen-ji|qq=}} on Mount Yoshino, three nearly identical statues of Zaō Gongen are enshrined together as the temple’s principal icons.
Under the {{ill|Shinbutsu Shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu Shūgō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=}} system, Zaō Gongen was identified with {{ill|Emperor Ankan|ja|安閑天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ankan|qq=}} (Hirokuni Oshitaka Kanehi-no-Mikoto), and during the {{ill|Meiji-era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|2=ja|3=明治|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji-era|qq=}} {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu Bunri|qq=}} policy, many shrines that previously worshipped Zaō Gongen reidentified their deities as Emperor Ankan.
In {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}, Zaō Gongen became syncretized with deities such as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=}}, {{ill|Kunitokotachi|ja|国之常立神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitokotachi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kanayamahiko|ja|金山彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanayamahiko|qq=}}. As a result, shrines enshrining Zaō Gongen often list these deities as their enshrined gods.
Zaō Gongen was also equated with {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|スサノオ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} at {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Taisha|qq=}} in the medieval period, particularly linked through the {{ill|Gakuen-ji|ja|鰐淵寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuen-ji|qq=}} temple.
== Iconography == === Appearance === Zaō Gongen is depicted similarly to {{ill|Vidyaraja|ja|明王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vidyaraja|qq=}}, with a fierce, wrathful expression and wild, upward-flowing hair. He usually holds a {{ill|vajra|ja|三鈷杵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/vajra|qq=}} in his right hand and forms a sword mudra with his left. His right leg is raised high, symbolizing the crushing of evil, while his left foot stands firmly on the ground. Flames blaze behind him, representing wisdom. Some statues, like those at {{ill|Kōryū-ji|ja|広隆寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōryū-ji|qq=}} in Kyoto, show both feet grounded, while others have variations with reversed postures.
One notable example is the main deity at {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|qq=}}’s {{ill|Nageire-dō|ja|投入堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nageire-dō|qq=}} in {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Prefecture|qq=}}, a designated Important Cultural Property from the Heian period.
=== Symbolism === The vajra represents the destruction of delusions, while the sword mudra symbolizes cutting through desires. His left foot suppresses demons, and the raised right foot disperses evil between heaven and earth. The flames signify enlightenment<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.22–25.</ref>.
== Zaō Mountain == {{ill|Mount Zaō|ja|蔵王山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Zaō|qq=}} spans {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|10=ja|11=宮城県|ja|宮城県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyagi Prefecture|qq=}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamagata Prefecture|qq=}}. Originally known as {{ill|Katta-mine|ja|刈田嶺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta-mine|qq=}} or {{ill|Mount Fubo|ja|不忘山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fubo|qq=}}, it was later named after Zaō Gongen when the deity was enshrined during the Heian period.
In the Edo period, it became a popular pilgrimage site. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}} and the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|1=Haibutsu kishaku|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haibutsu Kishaku|13=User:Immanuelle/Haibutsu kishaku|qq=}} movement, shrines like {{ill|Katta Mine Shrine|ja|刈田嶺神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta Mine Shrine|qq=}} redefined their enshrined deities, but the worship of Zaō Gongen persisted.
== Major Zaō Gongen Sites == * {{ill|Kinpusen-ji|ja|金峯山寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpusen-ji|qq=}} (Nara Prefecture) – Important Cultural Property * {{ill|Nyoi-in Temple|ja|如意輪寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoi-in Temple|qq=}} (Nara Prefecture) – Statue by the Kei school (1226 CE) * {{ill|Ishizuchi-san|ja|石鎚山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizuchi-san|qq=}} temples (Ehime Prefecture) * {{ill|Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|ja|大井蔵王権現神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|qq=}} (Tokyo)
== Mantras and Seed Syllables == * **Standard Mantra:** "On Bakiryū Sowaka" (オン・バキリュウ・ソワカ)<ref name="mantra">‘The Book of Mudras and Mantras,’ Gakken, 2004, p.152.</ref> * **Other Mantras:** "On Basakusha Aranja Sowaka," "On Basara Kusha Aranja Un Sowaka" * **Seed Syllable:** "हूं" (Hūṃ)<ref name="mantra"/>
== Zaō Gongen in Culture == * {{ill|Arashiyama (Noh)|ja|嵐山 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arashiyama (Noh)|qq=}} – Appears in Noh theatre * {{ill|Kusu (Noh)|ja|国栖 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusu (Noh)|qq=}} – Featured in Noh performance * {{ill|Hanagasa Ondo|ja|花笠音頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanagasa Ondo|qq=}} – Mentioned in folk songs == See Also == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Buddhas|qq=}}

It is considered a composite of {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|千手観音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon|qq=}}, and {{ill|Maitreya|ja|弥勒菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maitreya|qq=}}. At {{ill|Kinpusen-ji|ja|金峯山寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpusen-ji|qq=}} on Mount Yoshino, three nearly identical statues of Zaō Gongen are enshrined together as the temple’s principal icons.
Under the {{ill|Shinbutsu Shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu Shūgō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=}} system, Zaō Gongen was identified with {{ill|Emperor Ankan|ja|安閑天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ankan|qq=}} (Hirokuni Oshitaka Kanehi-no-Mikoto), and during the {{ill|Meiji-era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|2=ja|3=明治|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji-era|qq=}} {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu Bunri|qq=}} policy, many shrines that previously worshipped Zaō Gongen reidentified their deities as Emperor Ankan.
In {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}, Zaō Gongen became syncretized with deities such as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=}}, {{ill|Kunitokotachi|ja|国之常立神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitokotachi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kanayamahiko|ja|金山彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanayamahiko|qq=}}. Zaō Gongen was also equated with {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|スサノオ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} at {{ill|Izumo-taisha|lt=Izumo-taisha|lt=Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Taisha|qq=}} in the medieval period, particularly linked through the {{ill|Gakuen-ji|ja|鰐淵寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuen-ji|qq=}} temple.

He usually holds a {{ill|vajra|ja|三鈷杵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/vajra|qq=}} in his right hand and forms a sword mudra with his left. His right leg is raised high, symbolizing the crushing of evil, while his left foot stands firmly on the ground. Flames blaze behind him, representing wisdom. Some statues, like those at {{ill|Kōryū-ji|ja|広隆寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōryū-ji|qq=}} in Kyoto, show both feet grounded, while others have variations with reversed postures.
One notable example is the main deity at {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|qq=}}’s {{ill|Nageire-dō|ja|投入堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nageire-dō|qq=}} in {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Prefecture|qq=}}, a designated Important Cultural Property from the Heian period.

His left foot suppresses demons, and the raised right foot disperses evil between heaven and earth. The flames signify enlightenment<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.22–25.</ref>.
== Zaō Mountain == {{ill|Mount Zaō|ja|蔵王山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Zaō|qq=}} spans {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|10=ja|11=宮城県|ja|宮城県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyagi Prefecture|qq=}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamagata Prefecture|qq=}}. Originally known as {{ill|Katta-mine|ja|刈田嶺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta-mine|qq=}} or {{ill|Mount Fubo|ja|不忘山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fubo|qq=}}, it was later named after Zaō Gongen when the deity was enshrined during the Heian period.
In the Edo period, it became a popular pilgrimage site. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}} and the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|1=Haibutsu kishaku|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haibutsu Kishaku|13=User:Immanuelle/Haibutsu kishaku|qq=}} movement, shrines like {{ill|Katta Mine Shrine|ja|刈田嶺神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta Mine Shrine|qq=}} redefined their enshrined deities, but the worship of Zaō Gongen persisted.
== Major Zaō Gongen Sites == * {{ill|Kinpusen-ji|ja|金峯山寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpusen-ji|qq=}} (Nara Prefecture) – Important Cultural Property * {{ill|Nyoi-in Temple|ja|如意輪寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoi-in Temple|qq=}} (Nara Prefecture) – Statue by the Kei school (1226 CE) * {{ill|Ishizuchi-san|ja|石鎚山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizuchi-san|qq=}} temples (Ehime Prefecture) * {{ill|Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|ja|大井蔵王権現神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|qq=}} (Tokyo)
== Mantras and Seed Syllables == * **Standard Mantra:** "On Bakiryū Sowaka" (オン・バキリュウ・ソワカ)<ref name="mantra">‘The Book of Mudras and Mantras,’ Gakken, 2004, p.152.</ref> * **Other Mantras:** "On Basakusha Aranja Sowaka," "On Basara Kusha Aranja Un Sowaka" * **Seed Syllable:** "हूं" (Hūṃ)<ref name="mantra"/>
== Zaō Gongen in Culture == * {{ill|Arashiyama (Noh)|ja|嵐山 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arashiyama (Noh)|qq=}} – Appears in Noh theatre * {{ill|Kusu (Noh)|ja|国栖 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusu (Noh)|qq=}} – Featured in Noh performance * {{ill|Hanagasa Ondo|ja|花笠音頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanagasa Ondo|qq=}} – Mentioned in folk songs == See Also == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Buddhas|qq=}}

[[sn:Zaō Gongen]] [[en:Zaō Gongen]] [[de:Za%C5%8D Gongen]] [[zh:%E8%97%8F%E7%8E%8B%E6%AC%8A%E7%8F%BE]] [[cs:Za%C3%B3 gongen]] [[ja:蔵王権現]] [[sn:aō Gongen]]

[[File:蔵王権現立像-Zaō Gongen MET DT5160.jpg|thumb|銅造蔵王権現立像 [[メトロポリタン美術館]]蔵 平安時代（11世紀）<ref>所蔵先館のクレジットラインは以下のとおり。The Harry G. C. Packard Collection of Asian Art, Gift of Harry G. C. Packard, and Purchase, Fletcher, Rogers, Harris Brisbane Dick, and Louis V. Bell Funds, Joseph Pulitzer Bequest, and The Annenberg Fund Inc. Gift, 1975</ref>]] [[File:Zaou Gongen Circumstancial appearance of Mount Yoshino.jpg|thumb|蔵王権現（[[ギメ東洋美術館]]）]] [[File:ŌmineSanjō 2020a.jpg|thumb|湧出岩（[[山上ヶ岳]]）]]
'''Zaō Gongen''' (蔵王権現) is the principal deity of {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō|qq=}}, a form of Japanese mountain ascetic Buddhism. The formal name is '''Kongō Zaō Gongen''' (金剛蔵王権現) or '''Kongō Zaō Bosatsu''' (金剛蔵王菩薩)<ref>‘Konjaku Monogatari-shū,’ the legend of En no Gyōja.</ref>. This deity is uniquely Japanese, with no origins in Indian Buddhism, and is enshrined as the principal deity of {{ill|Kinpusen-ji|ja|金峯山寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpusen-ji|qq=}} in {{ill|Yoshino|ja|吉野町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshino|qq=}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Prefecture|qq=}}. The name "Kongō Zaō" signifies the ultimate, indestructible truth and the ruler governing all things<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction’ in ‘The Deity of Shugendō: Zaō Gongen,’ p.10–17.</ref>. The term {{ill|Gongen|ja|権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongen|qq=}} means “a deity manifesting temporarily in a provisional form.” Zaō Gongen is believed to encompass the powers of all {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhas|qq=}}, {{ill|Bodhisattvas|ja|菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bodhisattvas|qq=}}, deities, and spirits<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.18–21, p.26–28.</ref>.
== Origins == Zaō Gongen is said to have manifested to {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|qq=}}<ref>A mountain ascetic from the 7th century.</ref> during his training at {{ill|Mount Kinpu|ja|金峰山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kinpu|qq=}} in Yoshino<ref>Rinetaka Tanaka, ‘Introduction to Shugendō,’ Shueisha Shinsho, 2014, p.54.</ref>. It is considered a composite of {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|千手観音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon|qq=}}, and {{ill|Maitreya|ja|弥勒菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maitreya|qq=}}. At {{ill|Kinpusen-ji|ja|金峯山寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpusen-ji|qq=}} on Mount Yoshino, three nearly identical statues of Zaō Gongen are enshrined together as the temple’s principal icons.
Under the {{ill|Shinbutsu Shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu Shūgō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=}} system, Zaō Gongen was identified with {{ill|Emperor Ankan|ja|安閑天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ankan|qq=}} (Hirokuni Oshitaka Kanehi-no-Mikoto), and during the {{ill|Meiji-era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|2=ja|3=明治|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji-era|qq=}} {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu Bunri|qq=}} policy, many shrines that previously worshipped Zaō Gongen reidentified their deities as Emperor Ankan.
In {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}}, Zaō Gongen became syncretized with deities such as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=}}, {{ill|Kunitokotachi|ja|国之常立神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitokotachi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayamahiko|ja|金山彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanayamahiko|qq=}}. As a result, shrines enshrining Zaō Gongen often list these deities as their enshrined gods.
Zaō Gongen was also equated with {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|スサノオ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} at {{ill|Izumo-taisha|lt=Izumo-taisha|lt=Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Taisha|qq=}} in the medieval period, particularly linked through the {{ill|Gakuen-ji|ja|鰐淵寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuen-ji|qq=}} temple.
== Iconography == === Appearance === Zaō Gongen is depicted similarly to {{ill|Vidyaraja|ja|明王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vidyaraja|qq=}}, with a fierce, wrathful expression and wild, upward-flowing hair. He usually holds a {{ill|vajra|ja|三鈷杵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/vajra|qq=}} in his right hand and forms a sword mudra with his left. His right leg is raised high, symbolizing the crushing of evil, while his left foot stands firmly on the ground. Flames blaze behind him, representing wisdom. Some statues, like those at {{ill|Kōryū-ji|ja|広隆寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōryū-ji|qq=}} in Kyoto, show both feet grounded, while others have variations with reversed postures.
One notable example is the main deity at {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|qq=}}’s {{ill|Nageire-dō|ja|投入堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nageire-dō|qq=}} in {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Prefecture|qq=}}, a designated Important Cultural Property from the Heian period.
=== Symbolism === The vajra represents the destruction of delusions, while the sword mudra symbolizes cutting through desires. His left foot suppresses demons, and the raised right foot disperses evil between heaven and earth. The flames signify enlightenment<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.22–25.</ref>.
== Zaō Mountain == {{ill|Mount Zaō|ja|蔵王山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Zaō|qq=}} spans {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|10=ja|11=宮城県|ja|宮城県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyagi Prefecture|qq=}} and {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamagata Prefecture|qq=}}. Originally known as {{ill|Katta-mine|ja|刈田嶺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta-mine|qq=}} or {{ill|Mount Fubo|ja|不忘山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fubo|qq=}}, it was later named after Zaō Gongen when the deity was enshrined during the Heian period.
In the Edo period, it became a popular pilgrimage site. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}} and the {{ill|Haibutsu Kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|1=Haibutsu kishaku|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haibutsu Kishaku|13=User:Immanuelle/Haibutsu kishaku|qq=}} movement, shrines like {{ill|Katta Mine Shrine|ja|刈田嶺神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta Mine Shrine|qq=}} redefined their enshrined deities, but the worship of Zaō Gongen persisted.
== Major Zaō Gongen Sites == * {{ill|Kinpusen-ji|ja|金峯山寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinpusen-ji|qq=}} (Nara Prefecture) – Important Cultural Property * {{ill|Nyoi-in Temple|ja|如意輪寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoi-in Temple|qq=}} (Nara Prefecture) – Statue by the Kei school (1226 CE) * {{ill|Ishizuchi-san|ja|石鎚山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizuchi-san|qq=}} temples (Ehime Prefecture) * {{ill|Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|ja|大井蔵王権現神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|qq=}} (Tokyo)
== Mantras and Seed Syllables == * **Standard Mantra:** "On Bakiryū Sowaka" (オン・バキリュウ・ソワカ)<ref name="mantra">‘The Book of Mudras and Mantras,’ Gakken, 2004, p.152.</ref> * **Other Mantras:** "On Basakusha Aranja Sowaka," "On Basara Kusha Aranja Un Sowaka" * **Seed Syllable:** "हूं" (Hūṃ)<ref name="mantra"/>
== Zaō Gongen in Culture == * {{ill|Arashiyama (Noh)|ja|嵐山 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arashiyama (Noh)|qq=}} – Appears in Noh theatre * {{ill|Kusu (Noh)|ja|国栖 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusu (Noh)|qq=}} – Featured in Noh performance * {{ill|Hanagasa Ondo|ja|花笠音頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanagasa Ondo|qq=}} – Mentioned in folk songs == See Also == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Buddhas|qq=}}

[[File:蔵王権現立像-Zaō Gongen MET DT5160.jpg|thumb|銅造蔵王権現立像 [[メトロポリタン美術館]]蔵 平安時代（11世紀）<ref>所蔵先館のクレジットラインは以下のとおり。The Harry G. C. Packard Collection of Asian Art, Gift of Harry G. C. Packard, and Purchase, Fletcher, Rogers, Harris Brisbane Dick, and Louis V. Bell Funds, Joseph Pulitzer Bequest, and The Annenberg Fund Inc. Gift, 1975</ref>]] [[File:Zaou Gongen Circumstancial appearance of Mount Yoshino.jpg|thumb|蔵王権現（[[ギメ東洋美術館]]）]] [[File:ŌmineSanjō 2020a.jpg|thumb|湧出岩（[[山上ヶ岳]]）]]
'''Zaō Gongen''' (蔵王権現) is the principal deity of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], a form of Japanese mountain ascetic Buddhism. The formal name is '''Kongō Zaō Gongen''' (金剛蔵王権現) or '''Kongō Zaō Bosatsu''' (金剛蔵王菩薩)<ref>‘Konjaku Monogatari-shū,’ the legend of En no Gyōja.</ref>. This deity is uniquely Japanese, with no origins in Indian Buddhism, and is enshrined as the principal deity of [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The name "Kongō Zaō" signifies the ultimate, indestructible truth and the ruler governing all things<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction’ in ‘The Deity of Shugendō: Zaō Gongen,’ p.10–17.</ref>. The term [[:en:Gongen|Gongen]] means “a deity manifesting temporarily in a provisional form.” Zaō Gongen is believed to encompass the powers of all {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhas|qq=}}, [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattvas]], deities, and spirits<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.18–21, p.26–28.</ref>.
== Origins == Zaō Gongen is said to have manifested to [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]]<ref>A mountain ascetic from the 7th century.</ref> during his training at {{ill|Mount Kinpu|ja|金峰山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kinpu|qq=}} in Yoshino<ref>Rinetaka Tanaka, ‘Introduction to Shugendō,’ Shueisha Shinsho, 2014, p.54.</ref>. It is considered a composite of {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|千手観音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon|qq=}}, and [[:en:Maitreya|Maitreya]]. At [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] on Mount Yoshino, three nearly identical statues of Zaō Gongen are enshrined together as the temple’s principal icons.
Under the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] system, Zaō Gongen was identified with [[:en:Emperor Ankan|Emperor Ankan]] (Hirokuni Oshitaka Kanehi-no-Mikoto), and during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]] policy, many shrines that previously worshipped Zaō Gongen reidentified their deities as Emperor Ankan.
In [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]], Zaō Gongen became syncretized with deities such as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=}}, [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kunitokotachi]], [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]], and {{ill|Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayamahiko|ja|金山彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanayamahiko|qq=}}. As a result, shrines enshrining Zaō Gongen often list these deities as their enshrined gods.
Zaō Gongen was also equated with [[:en:Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] at [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] in the medieval period, particularly linked through the [[:en:Gakuen-ji|Gakuen-ji]] temple.
== Iconography == === Appearance === Zaō Gongen is depicted similarly to [[:en:Wisdom King|Vidyaraja]], with a fierce, wrathful expression and wild, upward-flowing hair. He usually holds a {{ill|vajra|ja|三鈷杵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/vajra|qq=}} in his right hand and forms a sword mudra with his left. His right leg is raised high, symbolizing the crushing of evil, while his left foot stands firmly on the ground. Flames blaze behind him, representing wisdom. Some statues, like those at [[:en:Kōryū-ji|Kōryū-ji]] in Kyoto, show both feet grounded, while others have variations with reversed postures.
One notable example is the main deity at {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|qq=}}’s {{ill|Nageire-dō|ja|投入堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nageire-dō|qq=}} in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]], a designated Important Cultural Property from the Heian period.
=== Symbolism === The vajra represents the destruction of delusions, while the sword mudra symbolizes cutting through desires. His left foot suppresses demons, and the raised right foot disperses evil between heaven and earth. The flames signify enlightenment<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.22–25.</ref>.
== Zaō Mountain == {{ill|Mount Zaō|ja|蔵王山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Zaō|qq=}} spans [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]. Originally known as {{ill|Katta-mine|ja|刈田嶺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta-mine|qq=}} or {{ill|Mount Fubo|ja|不忘山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fubo|qq=}}, it was later named after Zaō Gongen when the deity was enshrined during the Heian period.
In the Edo period, it became a popular pilgrimage site. During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement, shrines like {{ill|Katta Mine Shrine|ja|刈田嶺神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta Mine Shrine|qq=}} redefined their enshrined deities, but the worship of Zaō Gongen persisted.
== Major Zaō Gongen Sites == * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] (Nara Prefecture) – Important Cultural Property * {{ill|Nyoi-in Temple|ja|如意輪寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoi-in Temple|qq=}} (Nara Prefecture) – Statue by the Kei school (1226 CE) * [[:en:Mount Ishizuchi|Ishizuchi-san]] temples (Ehime Prefecture) * {{ill|Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|ja|大井蔵王権現神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|qq=}} (Tokyo)
== Mantras and Seed Syllables == * **Standard Mantra:** "On Bakiryū Sowaka" (オン・バキリュウ・ソワカ)<ref name="mantra">‘The Book of Mudras and Mantras,’ Gakken, 2004, p.152.</ref> * **Other Mantras:** "On Basakusha Aranja Sowaka," "On Basara Kusha Aranja Un Sowaka" * **Seed Syllable:** "हूं" (Hūṃ)<ref name="mantra"/>
== Zaō Gongen in Culture == * {{ill|Arashiyama (Noh)|ja|嵐山 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arashiyama (Noh)|qq=}} – Appears in Noh theatre * {{ill|Kusu (Noh)|ja|国栖 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusu (Noh)|qq=}} – Featured in Noh performance * {{ill|Hanagasa Ondo|ja|花笠音頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanagasa Ondo|qq=}} – Mentioned in folk songs == See Also == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Buddhas|qq=}}

revision.text:
[[File:蔵王権現立像-Zaō Gongen MET DT5160.jpg|thumb|銅造蔵王権現立像 [[メトロポリタン美術館]]蔵 平安時代（11世紀）<ref>所蔵先館のクレジットラインは以下のとおり。The Harry G. C. Packard Collection of Asian Art, Gift of Harry G. C. Packard, and Purchase, Fletcher, Rogers, Harris Brisbane Dick, and Louis V. Bell Funds, Joseph Pulitzer Bequest, and The Annenberg Fund Inc. Gift, 1975</ref>]] [[File:Zaou Gongen Circumstancial appearance of Mount Yoshino.jpg|thumb|蔵王権現（[[ギメ東洋美術館]]）]] [[File:ŌmineSanjō 2020a.jpg|thumb|湧出岩（[[山上ヶ岳]]）]]
'''Zaō Gongen''' (蔵王権現) is the principal deity of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], a form of Japanese mountain ascetic Buddhism. The formal name is '''Kongō Zaō Gongen''' (金剛蔵王権現) or '''Kongō Zaō Bosatsu''' (金剛蔵王菩薩)<ref>‘Konjaku Monogatari-shū,’ the legend of En no Gyōja.</ref>. This deity is uniquely Japanese, with no origins in Indian Buddhism, and is enshrined as the principal deity of [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The name "Kongō Zaō" signifies the ultimate, indestructible truth and the ruler governing all things<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction’ in ‘The Deity of Shugendō: Zaō Gongen,’ p.10–17.</ref>. The term [[:en:Gongen|Gongen]] means “a deity manifesting temporarily in a provisional form.” Zaō Gongen is believed to encompass the powers of all {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhas|qq=}}, [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattvas]], deities, and spirits<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.18–21, p.26–28.</ref>.
== Origins == Zaō Gongen is said to have manifested to [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]]<ref>A mountain ascetic from the 7th century.</ref> during his training at {{ill|Mount Kinpu|ja|金峰山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kinpu|qq=}} in Yoshino<ref>Rinetaka Tanaka, ‘Introduction to Shugendō,’ Shueisha Shinsho, 2014, p.54.</ref>. It is considered a composite of {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|千手観音|1=Sahasrabhuja|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Sahasrabhuja|qq=|6=ko|7=관세음보살|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon|qq=}}, and [[:en:Maitreya|Maitreya]]. At [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] on Mount Yoshino, three nearly identical statues of Zaō Gongen are enshrined together as the temple’s principal icons.
Under the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] system, Zaō Gongen was identified with [[:en:Emperor Ankan|Emperor Ankan]] (Hirokuni Oshitaka Kanehi-no-Mikoto), and during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]] policy, many shrines that previously worshipped Zaō Gongen reidentified their deities as Emperor Ankan.
In [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]], Zaō Gongen became syncretized with deities such as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=}}, [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kunitokotachi]], [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]], and {{ill|Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayamahiko|ja|金山彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanayamahiko|qq=}}. As a result, shrines enshrining Zaō Gongen often list these deities as their enshrined gods.
Zaō Gongen was also equated with [[:en:Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] at [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] in the medieval period, particularly linked through the [[:en:Gakuen-ji|Gakuen-ji]] temple.
== Iconography == === Appearance === Zaō Gongen is depicted similarly to [[:en:Wisdom King|Vidyaraja]], with a fierce, wrathful expression and wild, upward-flowing hair. He usually holds a {{ill|vajra|ja|三鈷杵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/vajra|qq=}} in his right hand and forms a sword mudra with his left. His right leg is raised high, symbolizing the crushing of evil, while his left foot stands firmly on the ground. Flames blaze behind him, representing wisdom. Some statues, like those at [[:en:Kōryū-ji|Kōryū-ji]] in Kyoto, show both feet grounded, while others have variations with reversed postures.
One notable example is the main deity at {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|qq=}}’s {{ill|Nageire-dō|ja|投入堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nageire-dō|qq=}} in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]], a designated Important Cultural Property from the Heian period.
=== Symbolism === The vajra represents the destruction of delusions, while the sword mudra symbolizes cutting through desires. His left foot suppresses demons, and the raised right foot disperses evil between heaven and earth. The flames signify enlightenment<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.22–25.</ref>.
== Zaō Mountain == {{ill|Mount Zaō|ja|蔵王山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Zaō|qq=}} spans [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]. Originally known as {{ill|Katta-mine|ja|刈田嶺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta-mine|qq=}} or {{ill|Mount Fubo|ja|不忘山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fubo|qq=}}, it was later named after Zaō Gongen when the deity was enshrined during the Heian period.
In the Edo period, it became a popular pilgrimage site. During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement, shrines like {{ill|Katta Mine Shrine|ja|刈田嶺神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta Mine Shrine|qq=}} redefined their enshrined deities, but the worship of Zaō Gongen persisted.
== Major Zaō Gongen Sites == * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] (Nara Prefecture) – Important Cultural Property * {{ill|Nyoi-in Temple|ja|如意輪寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoi-in Temple|qq=}} (Nara Prefecture) – Statue by the Kei school (1226 CE) * [[:en:Mount Ishizuchi|Ishizuchi-san]] temples (Ehime Prefecture) * {{ill|Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|ja|大井蔵王権現神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|qq=}} (Tokyo)
== Mantras and Seed Syllables == * **Standard Mantra:** "On Bakiryū Sowaka" (オン・バキリュウ・ソワカ)<ref name="mantra">‘The Book of Mudras and Mantras,’ Gakken, 2004, p.152.</ref> * **Other Mantras:** "On Basakusha Aranja Sowaka," "On Basara Kusha Aranja Un Sowaka" * **Seed Syllable:** "हूं" (Hūṃ)<ref name="mantra"/>
== Zaō Gongen in Culture == * {{ill|Arashiyama (Noh)|ja|嵐山 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arashiyama (Noh)|qq=}} – Appears in Noh theatre * {{ill|Kusu (Noh)|ja|国栖 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusu (Noh)|qq=}} – Featured in Noh performance * {{ill|Hanagasa Ondo|ja|花笠音頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanagasa Ondo|qq=}} – Mentioned in folk songs == See Also == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Buddhas|qq=}}
{{Shinto-Buddhism}} {{Medieval Shinto}}

Originally known as {{ill|Katta-mine|ja|刈田嶺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta-mine|qq=}} or {{ill|Mount Fubo|ja|不忘山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fubo|qq=}}, it was later named after Zaō Gongen when the deity was enshrined during the Heian period.
In the Edo period, it became a popular pilgrimage site. During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement, shrines like {{ill|Katta Mine Shrine|ja|刈田嶺神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta Mine Shrine|qq=}} redefined their enshrined deities, but the worship of Zaō Gongen persisted.
== Major Zaō Gongen Sites == * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] (Nara Prefecture) – Important Cultural Property * {{ill|Nyoi-in Temple|ja|如意輪寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoi-in Temple|qq=}} (Nara Prefecture) – Statue by the Kei school (1226 CE) * [[:en:Mount Ishizuchi|Ishizuchi-san]] temples (Ehime Prefecture) * {{ill|Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|ja|大井蔵王権現神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|qq=}} (Tokyo)
== Mantras and Seed Syllables == * **Standard Mantra:** "On Bakiryū Sowaka" (オン・バキリュウ・ソワカ)<ref name="mantra">‘The Book of Mudras and Mantras,’ Gakken, 2004, p.152.</ref> * **Other Mantras:** "On Basakusha Aranja Sowaka," "On Basara Kusha Aranja Un Sowaka" * **Seed Syllable:** "हूं" (Hūṃ)<ref name="mantra"/>

[[sn:Zaō Gongen]] [[en:Zaō Gongen]] [[de:Za%C5%8D Gongen]] [[zh:%E8%97%8F%E7%8E%8B%E6%AC%8A%E7%8F%BE]] [[cs:Za%C3%B3 gongen]] [[ja:蔵王権現]] [[sn:aō Gongen]] [[Category:Shugendō deities]] [[Category:天部]]

[[File:蔵王権現立像-Zaō Gongen MET DT5160.jpg|thumb|銅造蔵王権現立像 [[メトロポリタン美術館]]蔵 平安時代（11世紀）<ref>所蔵先館のクレジットラインは以下のとおり。The Harry G. C. Packard Collection of Asian Art, Gift of Harry G. C. Packard, and Purchase, Fletcher, Rogers, Harris Brisbane Dick, and Louis V. Bell Funds, Joseph Pulitzer Bequest, and The Annenberg Fund Inc. Gift, 1975</ref>]] [[File:Zaou Gongen Circumstancial appearance of Mount Yoshino.jpg|thumb|蔵王権現（[[ギメ東洋美術館]]）]] [[File:ŌmineSanjō 2020a.jpg|thumb|湧出岩（[[山上ヶ岳]]）]]
'''Zaō Gongen''' (蔵王権現) is the principal deity of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], a form of Japanese mountain ascetic Buddhism. The formal name is '''Kongō Zaō Gongen''' (金剛蔵王権現) or '''Kongō Zaō Bosatsu''' (金剛蔵王菩薩)<ref>‘Konjaku Monogatari-shū,’ the legend of En no Gyōja.</ref>. This deity is uniquely Japanese, with no origins in Indian Buddhism, and is enshrined as the principal deity of [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The name "Kongō Zaō" signifies the ultimate, indestructible truth and the ruler governing all things<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction’ in ‘The Deity of Shugendō: Zaō Gongen,’ p.10–17.</ref>. The term [[:en:Gongen|Gongen]] means “a deity manifesting temporarily in a provisional form.” Zaō Gongen is believed to encompass the powers of all {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhas|qq=}}, [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattvas]], deities, and spirits<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.18–21, p.26–28.</ref>.
== Origins == Zaō Gongen is said to have manifested to [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]]<ref>A mountain ascetic from the 7th century.</ref> during his training at {{ill|Mount Kinpu|ja|金峰山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kinpu|qq=}} in Yoshino<ref>Rinetaka Tanaka, ‘Introduction to Shugendō,’ Shueisha Shinsho, 2014, p.54.</ref>. It is considered a composite of {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|千手観音|1=Sahasrabhuja|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Sahasrabhuja|qq=|6=ko|7=관세음보살|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon|qq=}}, and [[:en:Maitreya|Maitreya]]. At [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] on Mount Yoshino, three nearly identical statues of Zaō Gongen are enshrined together as the temple’s principal icons.
Under the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] system, Zaō Gongen was identified with [[:en:Emperor Ankan|Emperor Ankan]] (Hirokuni Oshitaka Kanehi-no-Mikoto), and during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]] policy, many shrines that previously worshipped Zaō Gongen reidentified their deities as Emperor Ankan.
In [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]], Zaō Gongen became syncretized with deities such as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=}}, [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kunitokotachi]], [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]], and {{ill|Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayamahiko|ja|金山彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanayamahiko|qq=}}. As a result, shrines enshrining Zaō Gongen often list these deities as their enshrined gods.
Zaō Gongen was also equated with [[:en:Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] at [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] in the medieval period, particularly linked through the [[:en:Gakuen-ji|Gakuen-ji]] temple.
== Iconography == === Appearance === Zaō Gongen is depicted similarly to [[:en:Wisdom King|Vidyaraja]], with a fierce, wrathful expression and wild, upward-flowing hair. He usually holds a {{ill|vajra|ja|三鈷杵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/vajra|qq=}} in his right hand and forms a sword mudra with his left. His right leg is raised high, symbolizing the crushing of evil, while his left foot stands firmly on the ground. Flames blaze behind him, representing wisdom. Some statues, like those at [[:en:Kōryū-ji|Kōryū-ji]] in Kyoto, show both feet grounded, while others have variations with reversed postures.
One notable example is the main deity at {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|qq=}}’s {{ill|Nageire-dō|ja|投入堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nageire-dō|qq=}} in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]], a designated Important Cultural Property from the Heian period.
=== Symbolism === The vajra represents the destruction of delusions, while the sword mudra symbolizes cutting through desires. His left foot suppresses demons, and the raised right foot disperses evil between heaven and earth. The flames signify enlightenment<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.22–25.</ref>.
== Zaō Mountain == {{ill|Mount Zaō|ja|蔵王山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Zaō|qq=}} spans [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]. Originally known as {{ill|Katta-mine|ja|刈田嶺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta-mine|qq=}} or {{ill|Mount Fubo|ja|不忘山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fubo|qq=}}, it was later named after Zaō Gongen when the deity was enshrined during the Heian period.
In the Edo period, it became a popular pilgrimage site. During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement, shrines like {{ill|Katta Mine Shrine|ja|刈田嶺神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta Mine Shrine|qq=}} redefined their enshrined deities, but the worship of Zaō Gongen persisted.
== Major Zaō Gongen Sites == * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] (Nara Prefecture) – Important Cultural Property * {{ill|Nyoi-in Temple|ja|如意輪寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoi-in Temple|qq=}} (Nara Prefecture) – Statue by the Kei school (1226 CE) * [[:en:Mount Ishizuchi|Ishizuchi-san]] temples (Ehime Prefecture) * {{ill|Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|ja|大井蔵王権現神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|qq=}} (Tokyo)
== Mantras and Seed Syllables == * **Standard Mantra:** "On Bakiryū Sowaka" (オン・バキリュウ・ソワカ)<ref name="mantra">‘The Book of Mudras and Mantras,’ Gakken, 2004, p.152.</ref> * **Other Mantras:** "On Basakusha Aranja Sowaka," "On Basara Kusha Aranja Un Sowaka" * **Seed Syllable:** "हूं" (Hūṃ)<ref name="mantra"/>
== Zaō Gongen in Culture == * {{ill|Arashiyama (Noh)|ja|嵐山 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arashiyama (Noh)|qq=}} – Appears in Noh theatre * {{ill|Kusu (Noh)|ja|国栖 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusu (Noh)|qq=}} – Featured in Noh performance * {{ill|Hanagasa Ondo|ja|花笠音頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanagasa Ondo|qq=}} – Mentioned in folk songs == See Also == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Buddhas|qq=}}

The term [[:en:Gongen|Gongen]] means “a deity manifesting temporarily in a provisional form.” Zaō Gongen is believed to encompass the powers of all {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhas|qq=}}, [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattvas]], deities, and spirits<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.18–21, p.26–28.</ref>.

It is considered a composite of {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni|qq=}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|千手観音|1=Sahasrabhuja|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Sahasrabhuja|qq=|6=ko|7=관세음보살|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon|qq=}}, and [[:en:Maitreya|Maitreya]]. At [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] on Mount Yoshino, three nearly identical statues of Zaō Gongen are enshrined together as the temple’s principal icons.
Under the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] system, Zaō Gongen was identified with [[:en:Emperor Ankan|Emperor Ankan]] (Hirokuni Oshitaka Kanehi-no-Mikoto), and during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]] policy, many shrines that previously worshipped Zaō Gongen reidentified their deities as Emperor Ankan.
In [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]], Zaō Gongen became syncretized with deities such as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=}}, [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kunitokotachi]], [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]], and {{ill|Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayamahiko|ja|金山彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanayamahiko|qq=}}. As a result, shrines enshrining Zaō Gongen often list these deities as their enshrined gods.
Zaō Gongen was also equated with [[:en:Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] at [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] in the medieval period, particularly linked through the [[:en:Gakuen-ji|Gakuen-ji]] temple.
== Iconography == === Appearance === Zaō Gongen is depicted similarly to [[:en:Wisdom King|Vidyaraja]], with a fierce, wrathful expression and wild, upward-flowing hair. He usually holds a {{ill|vajra|ja|三鈷杵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/vajra|qq=}} in his right hand and forms a sword mudra with his left. His right leg is raised high, symbolizing the crushing of evil, while his left foot stands firmly on the ground. Flames blaze behind him, representing wisdom. Some statues, like those at [[:en:Kōryū-ji|Kōryū-ji]] in Kyoto, show both feet grounded, while others have variations with reversed postures.
One notable example is the main deity at {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|qq=}}’s {{ill|Nageire-dō|ja|投入堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nageire-dō|qq=}} in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]], a designated Important Cultural Property from the Heian period.
=== Symbolism === The vajra represents the destruction of delusions, while the sword mudra symbolizes cutting through desires. His left foot suppresses demons, and the raised right foot disperses evil between heaven and earth. The flames signify enlightenment<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.22–25.</ref>.
== Zaō Mountain == {{ill|Mount Zaō|ja|蔵王山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Zaō|qq=}} spans [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]. Originally known as {{ill|Katta-mine|ja|刈田嶺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta-mine|qq=}} or {{ill|Mount Fubo|ja|不忘山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fubo|qq=}}, it was later named after Zaō Gongen when the deity was enshrined during the Heian period.
In the Edo period, it became a popular pilgrimage site. During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement, shrines like {{ill|Katta Mine Shrine|ja|刈田嶺神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta Mine Shrine|qq=}} redefined their enshrined deities, but the worship of Zaō Gongen persisted.
== Major Zaō Gongen Sites == * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] (Nara Prefecture) – Important Cultural Property * {{ill|Nyoi-in Temple|ja|如意輪寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoi-in Temple|qq=}} (Nara Prefecture) – Statue by the Kei school (1226 CE) * [[:en:Mount Ishizuchi|Ishizuchi-san]] temples (Ehime Prefecture) * {{ill|Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|ja|大井蔵王権現神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|qq=}} (Tokyo)
== Mantras and Seed Syllables == * **Standard Mantra:** "On Bakiryū Sowaka" (オン・バキリュウ・ソワカ)<ref name="mantra">‘The Book of Mudras and Mantras,’ Gakken, 2004, p.152.</ref> * **Other Mantras:** "On Basakusha Aranja Sowaka," "On Basara Kusha Aranja Un Sowaka" * **Seed Syllable:** "हूं" (Hūṃ)<ref name="mantra"/>
== Zaō Gongen in Culture == * {{ill|Arashiyama (Noh)|ja|嵐山 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arashiyama (Noh)|qq=}} – Appears in Noh theatre * {{ill|Kusu (Noh)|ja|国栖 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusu (Noh)|qq=}} – Featured in Noh performance * {{ill|Hanagasa Ondo|ja|花笠音頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanagasa Ondo|qq=}} – Mentioned in folk songs == See Also == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Buddhas|qq=}}

Gift, 1975</ref>]] [[File:Zaou Gongen Circumstancial appearance of Mount Yoshino.jpg|thumb|蔵王権現（[[ギメ東洋美術館]]）]] [[File:ŌmineSanjō 2020a.jpg|thumb|湧出岩（[[山上ヶ岳]]）]]
'''Zaō Gongen''' (蔵王権現) is the principal deity of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], a form of Japanese mountain ascetic Buddhism. The formal name is '''Kongō Zaō Gongen''' (金剛蔵王権現) or '''Kongō Zaō Bosatsu''' (金剛蔵王菩薩)<ref>‘Konjaku Monogatari-shū,’ the legend of En no Gyōja.</ref>. This deity is uniquely Japanese, with no origins in Indian Buddhism, and is enshrined as the principal deity of [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The name "Kongō Zaō" signifies the ultimate, indestructible truth and the ruler governing all things<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction’ in ‘The Deity of Shugendō: Zaō Gongen,’ p.10–17.</ref>. The term [[:en:Gongen|Gongen]] means “a deity manifesting temporarily in a provisional form.” Zaō Gongen is believed to encompass the powers of all {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhas|qq=}}, [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattvas]], deities, and spirits<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.18–21, p.26–28.</ref>.

At [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] on Mount Yoshino, three nearly identical statues of Zaō Gongen are enshrined together as the temple’s principal icons.
Under the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] system, Zaō Gongen was identified with [[:en:Emperor Ankan|Emperor Ankan]] (Hirokuni Oshitaka Kanehi-no-Mikoto), and during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]] policy, many shrines that previously worshipped Zaō Gongen reidentified their deities as Emperor Ankan.
In [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]], Zaō Gongen became syncretized with deities such as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=}}, [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kunitokotachi]], [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]], and {{ill|Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayamahiko|ja|金山彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanayamahiko|qq=}}. As a result, shrines enshrining Zaō Gongen often list these deities as their enshrined gods.
Zaō Gongen was also equated with [[:en:Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] at [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] in the medieval period, particularly linked through the [[:en:Gakuen-ji|Gakuen-ji]] temple.

He usually holds a {{ill|vajra|ja|三鈷杵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/vajra|qq=}} in his right hand and forms a sword mudra with his left. His right leg is raised high, symbolizing the crushing of evil, while his left foot stands firmly on the ground. Flames blaze behind him, representing wisdom. Some statues, like those at [[:en:Kōryū-ji|Kōryū-ji]] in Kyoto, show both feet grounded, while others have variations with reversed postures.
One notable example is the main deity at {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|qq=}}’s {{ill|Nageire-dō|ja|投入堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nageire-dō|qq=}} in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]], a designated Important Cultural Property from the Heian period.

== Major Zaō Gongen Sites == * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] (Nara Prefecture) – Important Cultural Property * {{ill|Nyoi-in Temple|ja|如意輪寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoi-in Temple|qq=}} (Nara Prefecture) – Statue by the Kei school (1226 CE) * [[:en:Mount Ishizuchi|Ishizuchi-san]] temples (Ehime Prefecture) * {{ill|Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|ja|大井蔵王権現神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|qq=}} (Tokyo)
== Mantras and Seed Syllables == * **Standard Mantra:** "On Bakiryū Sowaka" (オン・バキリュウ・ソワカ)<ref name="mantra">‘The Book of Mudras and Mantras,’ Gakken, 2004, p.152.</ref> * **Other Mantras:** "On Basakusha Aranja Sowaka," "On Basara Kusha Aranja Un Sowaka" * **Seed Syllable:** "हूं" (Hūṃ)<ref name="mantra"/>
== Zaō Gongen in Culture == * {{ill|Arashiyama (Noh)|ja|嵐山 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arashiyama (Noh)|qq=}} – Appears in Noh theatre * {{ill|Kusu (Noh)|ja|国栖 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusu (Noh)|qq=}} – Featured in Noh performance * {{ill|Hanagasa Ondo|ja|花笠音頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanagasa Ondo|qq=}} – Mentioned in folk songs == See Also == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Buddhas|qq=}}

[[sn:Zaō Gongen]] [[en:Zaō Gongen]] [[de:Za%C5%8D Gongen]] [[zh:%E8%97%8F%E7%8E%8B%E6%AC%8A%E7%8F%BE]] [[cs:Za%C3%B3 gongen]] [[ja:蔵王権現]] [[sn:aō Gongen]] [[Category:Shugendō deities]] [[Category:天部]] [[Category:Vajrayana]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Heian period]] [[Category:En no Gyōja]] [[Category:Japanese Vajrayana Buddhism]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Meiji era]]
