C. Packard Collection of Asian Art, Gift of Harry G. C. Packard, and Purchase, Fletcher, Rogers, Harris Brisbane Dick, and Louis V. Bell Funds, Joseph Pulitzer Bequest, and The Annenberg Fund Inc. Gift, 1975</ref>]] [[File:Zaou Gongen Circumstancial appearance of Mount Yoshino.jpg|thumb|蔵王権現（[[ギメ東洋美術館]]）]] [[File:ŌmineSanjō 2020a.jpg|thumb|湧出岩（[[山上ヶ岳]]）]]
'''Zaō Gongen''' (蔵王権現) is the principal deity of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], a form of Japanese mountain ascetic Buddhism. The formal name is '''Kongō Zaō Gongen''' (金剛蔵王権現) or '''Kongō Zaō Bosatsu''' (金剛蔵王菩薩)<ref>‘Konjaku Monogatari-shū,’ the legend of En no Gyōja.</ref>. This deity is uniquely Japanese, with no origins in Indian Buddhism, and is enshrined as the principal deity of [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The name "Kongō Zaō" signifies the ultimate, indestructible truth and the ruler governing all things<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction’ in ‘The Deity of Shugendō: Zaō Gongen,’ p.10–17.</ref>. The term [[:en:Gongen|Gongen]] means “a deity manifesting temporarily in a provisional form.” Zaō Gongen is believed to encompass the powers of all {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhas|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仏陀}}, [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattvas]], deities, and spirits<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.18–21, p.26–28.</ref>.
== Origins == Zaō Gongen is said to have manifested to [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]]<ref>A mountain ascetic from the 7th century.</ref> during his training at {{ill|Mount Kinpu|ja|金峰山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kinpu|qq=}} in Yoshino<ref>Rinetaka Tanaka, ‘Introduction to Shugendō,’ Shueisha Shinsho, 2014, p.54.</ref>. It is considered a composite of {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|qq=|6=ko|7=관세음보살|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, and [[:en:Maitreya|Maitreya]]. At [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] on Mount Yoshino, three nearly identical statues of Zaō Gongen are enshrined together as the temple’s principal icons.
Under the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] system, Zaō Gongen was identified with [[:en:Emperor Ankan|Emperor Ankan]] (Hirokuni Oshitaka Kanehi-no-Mikoto), and during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]] policy, many shrines that previously worshipped Zaō Gongen reidentified their deities as Emperor Ankan.
In [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]], Zaō Gongen became syncretized with deities such as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kunitokotachi]], [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]], and {{ill|Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayamahiko|ja|金山彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanayamahiko|qq=}}. As a result, shrines enshrining Zaō Gongen often list these deities as their enshrined gods.
Zaō Gongen was also equated with [[:en:Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] at [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] in the medieval period, particularly linked through the [[:en:Gakuen-ji|Gakuen-ji]] temple.
== Iconography == === Appearance === Zaō Gongen is depicted similarly to [[:en:Wisdom King|Vidyaraja]], with a fierce, wrathful expression and wild, upward-flowing hair. He usually holds a {{ill|vajra|ja|三鈷杵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/vajra|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金剛杵}} in his right hand and forms a sword mudra with his left. His right leg is raised high, symbolizing the crushing of evil, while his left foot stands firmly on the ground. Flames blaze behind him, representing wisdom. Some statues, like those at [[:en:Kōryū-ji|Kōryū-ji]] in Kyoto, show both feet grounded, while others have variations with reversed postures.
One notable example is the main deity at {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三佛寺}}’s {{ill|Nageire-dō|ja|投入堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nageire-dō|qq=}} in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]], a designated Important Cultural Property from the Heian period.
=== Symbolism === The vajra represents the destruction of delusions, while the sword mudra symbolizes cutting through desires. His left foot suppresses demons, and the raised right foot disperses evil between heaven and earth. The flames signify enlightenment<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.22–25.</ref>.
== Zaō Mountain == {{ill|Mount Zaō|ja|蔵王山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Zaō|qq=}} spans [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]. Originally known as {{ill|Katta-mine|ja|刈田嶺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta-mine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Mount Fubo|ja|不忘山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fubo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 蔵王連峰}}, it was later named after Zaō Gongen when the deity was enshrined during the Heian period.
In the Edo period, it became a popular pilgrimage site. During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement, shrines like {{ill|Katta Mine Shrine|ja|刈田嶺神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta Mine Shrine|qq=}} redefined their enshrined deities, but the worship of Zaō Gongen persisted.
== Major Zaō Gongen Sites == * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] (Nara Prefecture) – Important Cultural Property * {{ill|Nyoi-in Temple|ja|如意輪寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoi-in Temple|qq=}} (Nara Prefecture) – Statue by the Kei school (1226 CE) * [[:en:Mount Ishizuchi|Ishizuchi-san]] temples (Ehime Prefecture) * {{ill|Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|ja|大井蔵王権現神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|qq=}} (Tokyo)
== Mantras and Seed Syllables == * **Standard Mantra:** "On Bakiryū Sowaka" (オン・バキリュウ・ソワカ)<ref name="mantra">‘The Book of Mudras and Mantras,’ Gakken, 2004, p.152.</ref> * **Other Mantras:** "On Basakusha Aranja Sowaka," "On Basara Kusha Aranja Un Sowaka" * **Seed Syllable:** "हूं" (Hūṃ)<ref name="mantra"/>
== Zaō Gongen in Culture == * {{ill|Arashiyama (Noh)|ja|嵐山 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arashiyama (Noh)|qq=}} – Appears in Noh theatre * {{ill|Kusu (Noh)|ja|国栖 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusu (Noh)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} – Featured in Noh performance * {{ill|Hanagasa Ondo|ja|花笠音頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanagasa Ondo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 花笠まつり}} – Mentioned in folk songs == See Also == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Buddhas|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の仏尊}}

At [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] on Mount Yoshino, three nearly identical statues of Zaō Gongen are enshrined together as the temple’s principal icons.
Under the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] system, Zaō Gongen was identified with [[:en:Emperor Ankan|Emperor Ankan]] (Hirokuni Oshitaka Kanehi-no-Mikoto), and during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]] policy, many shrines that previously worshipped Zaō Gongen reidentified their deities as Emperor Ankan.
In [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]], Zaō Gongen became syncretized with deities such as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kunitokotachi]], [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]], and {{ill|Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayamahiko|ja|金山彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanayamahiko|qq=}}. As a result, shrines enshrining Zaō Gongen often list these deities as their enshrined gods.
Zaō Gongen was also equated with [[:en:Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] at [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] in the medieval period, particularly linked through the [[:en:Gakuen-ji|Gakuen-ji]] temple.
== Iconography == === Appearance === Zaō Gongen is depicted similarly to [[:en:Wisdom King|Vidyaraja]], with a fierce, wrathful expression and wild, upward-flowing hair. He usually holds a {{ill|vajra|ja|三鈷杵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/vajra|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金剛杵}} in his right hand and forms a sword mudra with his left. His right leg is raised high, symbolizing the crushing of evil, while his left foot stands firmly on the ground. Flames blaze behind him, representing wisdom. Some statues, like those at [[:en:Kōryū-ji|Kōryū-ji]] in Kyoto, show both feet grounded, while others have variations with reversed postures.
One notable example is the main deity at {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三佛寺}}’s {{ill|Nageire-dō|ja|投入堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nageire-dō|qq=}} in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]], a designated Important Cultural Property from the Heian period.
=== Symbolism === The vajra represents the destruction of delusions, while the sword mudra symbolizes cutting through desires. His left foot suppresses demons, and the raised right foot disperses evil between heaven and earth. The flames signify enlightenment<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.22–25.</ref>.
== Zaō Mountain == {{ill|Mount Zaō|ja|蔵王山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Zaō|qq=}} spans [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]. Originally known as {{ill|Katta-mine|ja|刈田嶺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta-mine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Mount Fubo|ja|不忘山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fubo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 蔵王連峰}}, it was later named after Zaō Gongen when the deity was enshrined during the Heian period.
In the Edo period, it became a popular pilgrimage site. During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement, shrines like {{ill|Katta Mine Shrine|ja|刈田嶺神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta Mine Shrine|qq=}} redefined their enshrined deities, but the worship of Zaō Gongen persisted.
== Major Zaō Gongen Sites == * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] (Nara Prefecture) – Important Cultural Property * {{ill|Nyoi-in Temple|ja|如意輪寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoi-in Temple|qq=}} (Nara Prefecture) – Statue by the Kei school (1226 CE) * [[:en:Mount Ishizuchi|Ishizuchi-san]] temples (Ehime Prefecture) * {{ill|Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|ja|大井蔵王権現神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|qq=}} (Tokyo)
== Mantras and Seed Syllables == * **Standard Mantra:** "On Bakiryū Sowaka" (オン・バキリュウ・ソワカ)<ref name="mantra">‘The Book of Mudras and Mantras,’ Gakken, 2004, p.152.</ref> * **Other Mantras:** "On Basakusha Aranja Sowaka," "On Basara Kusha Aranja Un Sowaka" * **Seed Syllable:** "हूं" (Hūṃ)<ref name="mantra"/>

Gift, 1975</ref>]] [[File:Zaou Gongen Circumstancial appearance of Mount Yoshino.jpg|thumb|蔵王権現（[[ギメ東洋美術館]]）]] [[File:ŌmineSanjō 2020a.jpg|thumb|湧出岩（[[山上ヶ岳]]）]]
'''Zaō Gongen''' (蔵王権現) is the principal deity of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], a form of Japanese mountain ascetic Buddhism. The formal name is '''Kongō Zaō Gongen''' (金剛蔵王権現) or '''Kongō Zaō Bosatsu''' (金剛蔵王菩薩)<ref>‘Konjaku Monogatari-shū,’ the legend of En no Gyōja.</ref>. This deity is uniquely Japanese, with no origins in Indian Buddhism, and is enshrined as the principal deity of [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The name "Kongō Zaō" signifies the ultimate, indestructible truth and the ruler governing all things<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction’ in ‘The Deity of Shugendō: Zaō Gongen,’ p.10–17.</ref>. The term [[:en:Gongen|Gongen]] means “a deity manifesting temporarily in a provisional form.” Zaō Gongen is believed to encompass the powers of all {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhas|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仏陀}}, [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattvas]], deities, and spirits<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.18–21, p.26–28.</ref>.
== Origins == Zaō Gongen is said to have manifested to [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]]<ref>A mountain ascetic from the 7th century.</ref> during his training at {{ill|Mount Kinpu|ja|金峰山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kinpu|qq=}} in Yoshino<ref>Rinetaka Tanaka, ‘Introduction to Shugendō,’ Shueisha Shinsho, 2014, p.54.</ref>. It is considered a composite of {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|qq=|6=ko|7=관세음보살|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, and [[:en:Maitreya|Maitreya]]. At [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] on Mount Yoshino, three nearly identical statues of Zaō Gongen are enshrined together as the temple’s principal icons.
Under the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] system, Zaō Gongen was identified with [[:en:Emperor Ankan|Emperor Ankan]] (Hirokuni Oshitaka Kanehi-no-Mikoto), and during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]] policy, many shrines that previously worshipped Zaō Gongen reidentified their deities as Emperor Ankan.
In [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]], Zaō Gongen became syncretized with deities such as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kunitokotachi]], [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]], and {{ill|Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayamahiko|ja|金山彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanayamahiko|qq=}}. As a result, shrines enshrining Zaō Gongen often list these deities as their enshrined gods.
Zaō Gongen was also equated with [[:en:Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] at [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] in the medieval period, particularly linked through the [[:en:Gakuen-ji|Gakuen-ji]] temple.
== Iconography == === Appearance === Zaō Gongen is depicted similarly to [[:en:Wisdom King|Vidyaraja]], with a fierce, wrathful expression and wild, upward-flowing hair. He usually holds a {{ill|vajra|ja|三鈷杵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/vajra|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金剛杵}} in his right hand and forms a sword mudra with his left. His right leg is raised high, symbolizing the crushing of evil, while his left foot stands firmly on the ground. Flames blaze behind him, representing wisdom. Some statues, like those at [[:en:Kōryū-ji|Kōryū-ji]] in Kyoto, show both feet grounded, while others have variations with reversed postures.
One notable example is the main deity at {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三佛寺}}’s {{ill|Nageire-dō|ja|投入堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nageire-dō|qq=}} in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]], a designated Important Cultural Property from the Heian period.
=== Symbolism === The vajra represents the destruction of delusions, while the sword mudra symbolizes cutting through desires. His left foot suppresses demons, and the raised right foot disperses evil between heaven and earth. The flames signify enlightenment<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.22–25.</ref>.
== Zaō Mountain == {{ill|Mount Zaō|ja|蔵王山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Zaō|qq=}} spans [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]. Originally known as {{ill|Katta-mine|ja|刈田嶺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta-mine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Mount Fubo|ja|不忘山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fubo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 蔵王連峰}}, it was later named after Zaō Gongen when the deity was enshrined during the Heian period.
In the Edo period, it became a popular pilgrimage site. During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement, shrines like {{ill|Katta Mine Shrine|ja|刈田嶺神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta Mine Shrine|qq=}} redefined their enshrined deities, but the worship of Zaō Gongen persisted.

== Iconography == === Appearance === Zaō Gongen is depicted similarly to [[:en:Wisdom King|Vidyaraja]], with a fierce, wrathful expression and wild, upward-flowing hair. He usually holds a {{ill|vajra|ja|三鈷杵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/vajra|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金剛杵|1=vajra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q695709}} in his right hand and forms a sword mudra with his left. His right leg is raised high, symbolizing the crushing of evil, while his left foot stands firmly on the ground. Flames blaze behind him, representing wisdom. Some statues, like those at [[:en:Kōryū-ji|Kōryū-ji]] in Kyoto, show both feet grounded, while others have variations with reversed postures.
One notable example is the main deity at {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三佛寺|1=Sanbutsu-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3471689}}’s {{ill|Nageire-dō|ja|投入堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nageire-dō|qq=|1=Nageire-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q19826408}} in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]], a designated Important Cultural Property from the Heian period.
=== Symbolism === The vajra represents the destruction of delusions, while the sword mudra symbolizes cutting through desires. His left foot suppresses demons, and the raised right foot disperses evil between heaven and earth. The flames signify enlightenment<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.22–25.</ref>.
== Zaō Mountain == {{ill|Mount Zaō|ja|蔵王山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Zaō|qq=|1=Mount Zaō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621938}} spans [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]. Originally known as {{ill|Katta-mine|ja|刈田嶺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta-mine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Mount Fubo|ja|不忘山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fubo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 蔵王連峰|1=Mount Zaō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q167951}}, it was later named after Zaō Gongen when the deity was enshrined during the Heian period.
In the Edo period, it became a popular pilgrimage site. During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement, shrines like {{ill|Katta Mine Shrine|ja|刈田嶺神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta Mine Shrine|qq=|1=Katta Mine Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396253}} redefined their enshrined deities, but the worship of Zaō Gongen persisted.
== Major Zaō Gongen Sites == * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] (Nara Prefecture) – Important Cultural Property * {{ill|Nyoi-in Temple|ja|如意輪寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoi-in Temple|qq=|1=Nyoirin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213767}} (Nara Prefecture) – Statue by the Kei school (1226 CE) * [[:en:Mount Ishizuchi|Ishizuchi-san]] temples (Ehime Prefecture) * {{ill|Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|ja|大井蔵王権現神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|qq=|1=Oizōō Kengengen Jinja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q43594517}} (Tokyo)
== Mantras and Seed Syllables == * **Standard Mantra:** "On Bakiryū Sowaka" (オン・バキリュウ・ソワカ)<ref name="mantra">‘The Book of Mudras and Mantras,’ Gakken, 2004, p.152.</ref> * **Other Mantras:** "On Basakusha Aranja Sowaka," "On Basara Kusha Aranja Un Sowaka" * **Seed Syllable:** "हूं" (Hūṃ)<ref name="mantra"/>

[[sn:Zaō Gongen]] [[en:Zaō Gongen]] [[de:Za%C5%8D Gongen]] [[zh:%E8%97%8F%E7%8E%8B%E6%AC%8A%E7%8F%BE]] [[cs:Za%C3%B3 gongen]] [[ja:蔵王権現]] [[sn:aō Gongen]] [[Category:Shugendō deities]] [[Category:Deva (Buddhism)]] [[Category:Vajrayana]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Heian period]] [[Category:En no Gyōja]] [[Category:Japanese Vajrayana Buddhism]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Meiji era]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Edo period]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Religion in Japan]] [[Category:Synkretistische Religion]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Zao gongen}} [[cs:Zaó gongen]] [[de:Zaō Gongen]] [[zh:藏王權現]] <!--dewiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Zao gongen}} [[cs:Zaó gongen]] [[de:Zaō Gongen]] [[zh:藏王權現]] <!--zhwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Zao gongen}} [[cs:Zaó gongen]] [[de:Zaō Gongen]] [[zh:藏王權現]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Zaō Gongen]][[Category:qq]]

{{Infobox deity|image=File:Zao-Gongen-Kimpusen-Temple-Yoshino.jpg|venerated_in={{ill|Shugendō|qid=Q1483875}} |associated_deities ={{ill|Maitreya|qid=Q193461}}; {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qid=Q11644537}}; {{ill|Thousand-Armed Thousand-Eyed Avalokiteśvara|qid=Q10904910}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q10514059]] Wikidata name: Zaō Gongen [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:蔵王権現立像-Zaō Gongen MET DT5160.jpg|thumb|銅造蔵王権現立像 [[メトロポリタン美術館]]蔵 平安時代（11世紀）<ref>所蔵先館のクレジットラインは以下のとおり。The Harry G. C. Packard Collection of Asian Art, Gift of Harry G. C. Packard, and Purchase, Fletcher, Rogers, Harris Brisbane Dick, and Louis V. Bell Funds, Joseph Pulitzer Bequest, and The Annenberg Fund Inc. Gift, 1975</ref>]] [[File:Zaou Gongen Circumstancial appearance of Mount Yoshino.jpg|thumb|蔵王権現（[[ギメ東洋美術館]]）]] [[File:ŌmineSanjō 2020a.jpg|thumb|湧出岩（[[山上ヶ岳]]）]]
'''Zaō Gongen''' (蔵王権現) is the principal deity of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], a form of Japanese mountain ascetic Buddhism. The formal name is '''Kongō Zaō Gongen''' (金剛蔵王権現) or '''Kongō Zaō Bosatsu''' (金剛蔵王菩薩)<ref>‘Konjaku Monogatari-shū,’ the legend of En no Gyōja.</ref>. This deity is uniquely Japanese, with no origins in Indian Buddhism, and is enshrined as the principal deity of [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The name "Kongō Zaō" signifies the ultimate, indestructible truth and the ruler governing all things<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction’ in ‘The Deity of Shugendō: Zaō Gongen,’ p.10–17.</ref>. The term [[:en:Gongen|Gongen]] means “a deity manifesting temporarily in a provisional form.” Zaō Gongen is believed to encompass the powers of all {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhas|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仏陀|1=Buddha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7055}}, [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattvas]], deities, and spirits<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.18–21, p.26–28.</ref>.
== Origins == Zaō Gongen is said to have manifested to [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]]<ref>A mountain ascetic from the 7th century.</ref> during his training at {{ill|Mount Kinpu|ja|金峰山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kinpu|qq=|1=Mount Kinpusen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10563986}} in Yoshino<ref>Rinetaka Tanaka, ‘Introduction to Shugendō,’ Shueisha Shinsho, 2014, p.54.</ref>. It is considered a composite of {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|qq=|6=ko|7=관세음보살|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, and [[:en:Maitreya|Maitreya]]. At [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] on Mount Yoshino, three nearly identical statues of Zaō Gongen are enshrined together as the temple’s principal icons.
Under the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] system, Zaō Gongen was identified with [[:en:Emperor Ankan|Emperor Ankan]] (Hirokuni Oshitaka Kanehi-no-Mikoto), and during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]] policy, many shrines that previously worshipped Zaō Gongen reidentified their deities as Emperor Ankan.
In [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]], Zaō Gongen became syncretized with deities such as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kunitokotachi]], [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]], and {{ill|Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayamahiko|ja|金山彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanayamahiko|qq=}}. As a result, shrines enshrining Zaō Gongen often list these deities as their enshrined gods.
Zaō Gongen was also equated with [[:en:Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] at [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] in the medieval period, particularly linked through the [[:en:Gakuen-ji|Gakuen-ji]] temple.
== Iconography == === Appearance === Zaō Gongen is depicted similarly to [[:en:Wisdom King|Vidyaraja]], with a fierce, wrathful expression and wild, upward-flowing hair. He usually holds a {{ill|vajra|ja|三鈷杵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/vajra|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金剛杵|1=vajra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q695709}} in his right hand and forms a sword mudra with his left. His right leg is raised high, symbolizing the crushing of evil, while his left foot stands firmly on the ground. Flames blaze behind him, representing wisdom. Some statues, like those at [[:en:Kōryū-ji|Kōryū-ji]] in Kyoto, show both feet grounded, while others have variations with reversed postures.
One notable example is the main deity at {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三佛寺|1=Sanbutsu-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3471689}}’s {{ill|Nageire-dō|ja|投入堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nageire-dō|qq=|1=Nageire-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q19826408}} in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]], a designated Important Cultural Property from the Heian period.
=== Symbolism === The vajra represents the destruction of delusions, while the sword mudra symbolizes cutting through desires. His left foot suppresses demons, and the raised right foot disperses evil between heaven and earth. The flames signify enlightenment<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.22–25.</ref>.
== Zaō Mountain == {{ill|Mount Zaō|ja|蔵王山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Zaō|qq=|1=Mount Zaō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621938}} spans [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]. Originally known as {{ill|Katta-mine|ja|刈田嶺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta-mine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Mount Fubo|ja|不忘山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fubo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 蔵王連峰|1=Mount Zaō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q167951}}, it was later named after Zaō Gongen when the deity was enshrined during the Heian period.
In the Edo period, it became a popular pilgrimage site. During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement, shrines like {{ill|Katta Mine Shrine|ja|刈田嶺神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta Mine Shrine|qq=|1=Katta Mine Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396253}} redefined their enshrined deities, but the worship of Zaō Gongen persisted.
== Major Zaō Gongen Sites == * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] (Nara Prefecture) – Important Cultural Property * {{ill|Nyoi-in Temple|ja|如意輪寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoi-in Temple|qq=|1=Nyoirin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213767}} (Nara Prefecture) – Statue by the Kei school (1226 CE) * [[:en:Mount Ishizuchi|Ishizuchi-san]] temples (Ehime Prefecture) * {{ill|Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|ja|大井蔵王権現神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|qq=|1=Ōi-zaō Gongen Jinja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q43594517}} (Tokyo)
== Mantras and Seed Syllables == * **Standard Mantra:** "On Bakiryū Sowaka" (オン・バキリュウ・ソワカ)<ref name="mantra">‘The Book of Mudras and Mantras,’ Gakken, 2004, p.152.</ref> * **Other Mantras:** "On Basakusha Aranja Sowaka," "On Basara Kusha Aranja Un Sowaka" * **Seed Syllable:** "हूं" (Hūṃ)<ref name="mantra"/>

Zaō Gongen was also equated with [[:en:Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] at [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] in the medieval period, particularly linked through the [[:en:Gakuen-ji|Gakuen-ji]] temple.

He usually holds a {{ill|vajra|ja|三鈷杵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/vajra|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金剛杵|1=vajra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q695709}} in his right hand and forms a sword mudra with his left. His right leg is raised high, symbolizing the crushing of evil, while his left foot stands firmly on the ground. Flames blaze behind him, representing wisdom. Some statues, like those at [[:en:Kōryū-ji|Kōryū-ji]] in Kyoto, show both feet grounded, while others have variations with reversed postures.
One notable example is the main deity at {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三佛寺|1=Sanbutsu-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3471689}}’s {{ill|Nageire-dō|ja|投入堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nageire-dō|qq=|1=Nageire-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q19826408}} in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]], a designated Important Cultural Property from the Heian period.
=== Symbolism === The vajra represents the destruction of delusions, while the sword mudra symbolizes cutting through desires. His left foot suppresses demons, and the raised right foot disperses evil between heaven and earth. The flames signify enlightenment<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.22–25.</ref>.
== Zaō Mountain == {{ill|Mount Zaō|ja|蔵王山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Zaō|qq=|1=Mount Zaō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621938}} spans [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]. Originally known as {{ill|Katta-mine|ja|刈田嶺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta-mine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Mount Fubo|ja|不忘山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fubo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 蔵王連峰|1=Mount Zaō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q167951}}, it was later named after Zaō Gongen when the deity was enshrined during the Heian period.
In the Edo period, it became a popular pilgrimage site. During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement, shrines like {{ill|Katta Mine Shrine|ja|刈田嶺神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta Mine Shrine|qq=|1=Katta Mine Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396253}} redefined their enshrined deities, but the worship of Zaō Gongen persisted.
== Major Zaō Gongen Sites == * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] (Nara Prefecture) – Important Cultural Property * {{ill|Nyoi-in Temple|ja|如意輪寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoi-in Temple|qq=|1=Nyoirin-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213767}} (Nara Prefecture) – Statue by the Kei school (1226 CE) * [[:en:Mount Ishizuchi|Ishizuchi-san]] temples (Ehime Prefecture) * {{ill|Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|ja|大井蔵王権現神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|qq=|1=Ōi-zaō Gongen Jinja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q43594517}} (Tokyo)
== Mantras and Seed Syllables == * **Standard Mantra:** "On Bakiryū Sowaka" (オン・バキリュウ・ソワカ)<ref name="mantra">‘The Book of Mudras and Mantras,’ Gakken, 2004, p.152.</ref> * **Other Mantras:** "On Basakusha Aranja Sowaka," "On Basara Kusha Aranja Un Sowaka" * **Seed Syllable:** "हूं" (Hūṃ)<ref name="mantra"/>
== Zaō Gongen in Culture == * {{ill|Arashiyama (Noh)|ja|嵐山 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arashiyama (Noh)|qq=|1=Arashiyama (Noh)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17988500}} – Appears in Noh theatre * {{ill|Kusu (Noh)|ja|国栖 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusu (Noh)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} – Featured in Noh performance * {{ill|Hanagasa Ondo|ja|花笠音頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanagasa Ondo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 花笠まつり|1=hanagasa festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109930449}} – Mentioned in folk songs == See Also == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Buddhas|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の仏尊|1=Japanese Buddhist pantheon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6158131}}

[[sn:Zaō Gongen]] [[en:Zaō Gongen]] [[de:Za%C5%8D Gongen]] [[zh:%E8%97%8F%E7%8E%8B%E6%AC%8A%E7%8F%BE]] [[cs:Za%C3%B3 gongen]] [[ja:蔵王権現]] [[sn:aō Gongen]] [[Category:Shugendō deities]] [[Category:Deva (Buddhism)]] [[Category:Vajrayana]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Heian period]] [[Category:En no Gyōja]] [[Category:Japanese Vajrayana Buddhism]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Meiji era]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Edo period]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Religion in Japan]] [[Category:Synkretistische Religion]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Zao gongen}} [[cs:Zaó gongen]] [[de:Zaō Gongen]] [[zh:藏王權現]] <!--dewiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Zao gongen}} [[cs:Zaó gongen]] [[de:Zaō Gongen]] [[zh:藏王權現]] <!--zhwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Zao gongen}} [[cs:Zaó gongen]] [[de:Zaō Gongen]] [[zh:藏王權現]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[en:Zaō Gongen]]

{{Infobox deity|image=File:Zao-Gongen-Kimpusen-Temple-Yoshino.jpg|venerated_in={{ill|Shugendō|qid=Q1483875}} |associated_deities ={{ill|Maitreya|qid=Q193461}}; {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qid=Q11644537}}; {{ill|Thousand-Armed Thousand-Eyed Avalokiteśvara|qid=Q10904910}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q10514059]] Wikidata name: Zaō Gongen [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:蔵王権現立像-Zaō Gongen MET DT5160.jpg|thumb|銅造蔵王権現立像 [[メトロポリタン美術館]]蔵 平安時代（11世紀）<ref>所蔵先館のクレジットラインは以下のとおり。The Harry G. C. Packard Collection of Asian Art, Gift of Harry G. C. Packard, and Purchase, Fletcher, Rogers, Harris Brisbane Dick, and Louis V. Bell Funds, Joseph Pulitzer Bequest, and The Annenberg Fund Inc. Gift, 1975</ref>]] [[File:Zaou Gongen Circumstancial appearance of Mount Yoshino.jpg|thumb|蔵王権現（[[ギメ東洋美術館]]）]] [[File:ŌmineSanjō 2020a.jpg|thumb|湧出岩（[[山上ヶ岳]]）]]
'''Zaō Gongen''' (蔵王権現) is the principal deity of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], a form of Japanese mountain ascetic Buddhism. The formal name is '''Kongō Zaō Gongen''' (金剛蔵王権現) or '''Kongō Zaō Bosatsu''' (金剛蔵王菩薩)<ref>‘Konjaku Monogatari-shū,’ the legend of En no Gyōja.</ref>. This deity is uniquely Japanese, with no origins in Indian Buddhism, and is enshrined as the principal deity of [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The name "Kongō Zaō" signifies the ultimate, indestructible truth and the ruler governing all things<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction’ in ‘The Deity of Shugendō: Zaō Gongen,’ p.10–17.</ref>. The term [[:en:Gongen|Gongen]] means “a deity manifesting temporarily in a provisional form.” Zaō Gongen is believed to encompass the powers of all {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhas|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仏陀|1=Buddha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7055}}, [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattvas]], deities, and spirits<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.18–21, p.26–28.</ref>.
== Origins == Zaō Gongen is said to have manifested to [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]]<ref>A mountain ascetic from the 7th century.</ref> during his training at {{ill|Mount Kinpu|ja|金峰山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kinpu|qq=|1=Mount Kinpusen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10563986}} in Yoshino<ref>Rinetaka Tanaka, ‘Introduction to Shugendō,’ Shueisha Shinsho, 2014, p.54.</ref>. It is considered a composite of {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|qq=|6=ko|7=관세음보살|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, and [[:en:Maitreya|Maitreya]]. At [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] on Mount Yoshino, three nearly identical statues of Zaō Gongen are enshrined together as the temple’s principal icons.
Under the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] system, Zaō Gongen was identified with [[:en:Emperor Ankan|Emperor Ankan]] (Hirokuni Oshitaka Kanehi-no-Mikoto), and during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]] policy, many shrines that previously worshipped Zaō Gongen reidentified their deities as Emperor Ankan.
In [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]], Zaō Gongen became syncretized with deities such as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kunitokotachi]], [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]], and {{ill|Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayamahiko|ja|金山彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanayamahiko|qq=}}. As a result, shrines enshrining Zaō Gongen often list these deities as their enshrined gods.
Zaō Gongen was also equated with [[:en:Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] at [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] in the medieval period, particularly linked through the [[:en:Gakuen-ji|Gakuen-ji]] temple.
== Iconography == === Appearance === Zaō Gongen is depicted similarly to [[:en:Wisdom King|Vidyaraja]], with a fierce, wrathful expression and wild, upward-flowing hair. He usually holds a {{ill|vajra|ja|三鈷杵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/vajra|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金剛杵|1=vajra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q695709}} in his right hand and forms a sword mudra with his left. His right leg is raised high, symbolizing the crushing of evil, while his left foot stands firmly on the ground. Flames blaze behind him, representing wisdom. Some statues, like those at [[:en:Kōryū-ji|Kōryū-ji]] in Kyoto, show both feet grounded, while others have variations with reversed postures.
One notable example is the main deity at {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三佛寺|1=Sanbutsu-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3471689}}’s {{ill|Nageire-dō|ja|投入堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nageire-dō|qq=|1=Nageire-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q19826408}} in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]], a designated Important Cultural Property from the Heian period.
=== Symbolism === The vajra represents the destruction of delusions, while the sword mudra symbolizes cutting through desires. His left foot suppresses demons, and the raised right foot disperses evil between heaven and earth. The flames signify enlightenment<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.22–25.</ref>.
== Zaō Mountain == {{ill|Mount Zaō|ja|蔵王山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Zaō|qq=|1=Mount Zaō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621938}} spans [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]. Originally known as {{ill|Katta-mine|ja|刈田嶺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta-mine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Mount Fubo|ja|不忘山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fubo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 蔵王連峰|1=Mount Zaō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q167951}}, it was later named after Zaō Gongen when the deity was enshrined during the Heian period.
In the Edo period, it became a popular pilgrimage site. During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement, shrines like {{ill|Katta Mine Shrine|ja|刈田嶺神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta Mine Shrine|qq=|1=Katta Mine Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396253}} redefined their enshrined deities, but the worship of Zaō Gongen persisted.
== Major Zaō Gongen Sites == * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] (Nara Prefecture) – Important Cultural Property * {{ill|Nyoi-in Temple|ja|如意輪寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoi-in Temple|qq=|1=Nyoirin-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213767}} (Nara Prefecture) – Statue by the Kei school (1226 CE) * [[:en:Mount Ishizuchi|Ishizuchi-san]] temples (Ehime Prefecture) * {{ill|Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|ja|大井蔵王権現神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|qq=|1=Ōi-zaō Gongen Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q43594517}} (Tokyo)
== Mantras and Seed Syllables == * **Standard Mantra:** "On Bakiryū Sowaka" (オン・バキリュウ・ソワカ)<ref name="mantra">‘The Book of Mudras and Mantras,’ Gakken, 2004, p.152.</ref> * **Other Mantras:** "On Basakusha Aranja Sowaka," "On Basara Kusha Aranja Un Sowaka" * **Seed Syllable:** "हूं" (Hūṃ)<ref name="mantra"/>
== Zaō Gongen in Culture == * {{ill|Arashiyama (Noh)|ja|嵐山 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arashiyama (Noh)|qq=|1=Arashiyama (Noh)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17988500}} – Appears in Noh theatre * {{ill|Kusu (Noh)|ja|国栖 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusu (Noh)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} – Featured in Noh performance * {{ill|Hanagasa Ondo|ja|花笠音頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanagasa Ondo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 花笠まつり|1=hanagasa festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109930449}} – Mentioned in folk songs == See Also == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Buddhas|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の仏尊|1=Japanese Buddhist pantheon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6158131}}

'''Zaō Gongen''' (蔵王権現) is the principal deity of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], a form of Japanese mountain ascetic Buddhism. The formal name is '''Kongō Zaō Gongen''' (金剛蔵王権現) or '''Kongō Zaō Bosatsu''' (金剛蔵王菩薩)<ref>‘Konjaku Monogatari-shū,’ the legend of En no Gyōja.</ref>. The name "Kongō Zaō" signifies the ultimate, indestructible truth and the ruler governing all things<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction’ in ‘The Deity of Shugendō: Zaō Gongen,’ p.10–17.</ref>. The term [[:en:Gongen|Gongen]] means “a deity manifesting temporarily in a provisional form.” Zaō Gongen is believed to encompass the powers of all {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhas|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仏陀|1=Buddha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7055}}, [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattvas]], deities, and spirits<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.18–21, p.26–28.</ref>.
== Origins == Zaō Gongen is said to have manifested to [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]]<ref>A mountain ascetic from the 7th century.</ref> during his training at {{ill|Mount Kinpu|ja|金峰山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kinpu|qq=|1=Mount Kinpusen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10563986}} in Yoshino<ref>Rinetaka Tanaka, ‘Introduction to Shugendō,’ Shueisha Shinsho, 2014, p.54.</ref>. It is considered a composite of {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|qq=|6=ko|7=관세음보살|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, and [[:en:Maitreya|Maitreya]]. At [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] on Mount Yoshino, three nearly identical statues of Zaō Gongen are enshrined together as the temple’s principal icons.

As a result, shrines enshrining Zaō Gongen often list these deities as their enshrined gods.
Zaō Gongen was also equated with [[:en:Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] at [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] in the medieval period, particularly linked through the [[:en:Gakuen-ji|Gakuen-ji]] temple.
== Iconography == === Appearance === Zaō Gongen is depicted similarly to [[:en:Wisdom King|Vidyaraja]], with a fierce, wrathful expression and wild, upward-flowing hair. He usually holds a {{ill|vajra|ja|三鈷杵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/vajra|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金剛杵|1=vajra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q695709}} in his right hand and forms a sword mudra with his left. His right leg is raised high, symbolizing the crushing of evil, while his left foot stands firmly on the ground. Flames blaze behind him, representing wisdom. Some statues, like those at [[:en:Kōryū-ji|Kōryū-ji]] in Kyoto, show both feet grounded, while others have variations with reversed postures.
One notable example is the main deity at {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三佛寺|1=Sanbutsu-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3471689}}’s {{ill|Nageire-dō|ja|投入堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nageire-dō|qq=|1=Nageire-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q19826408}} in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]], a designated Important Cultural Property from the Heian period.
=== Symbolism === The vajra represents the destruction of delusions, while the sword mudra symbolizes cutting through desires. His left foot suppresses demons, and the raised right foot disperses evil between heaven and earth. The flames signify enlightenment<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.22–25.</ref>.
== Zaō Mountain == {{ill|Mount Zaō|ja|蔵王山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Zaō|qq=|1=Mount Zaō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621938}} spans [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]. Originally known as {{ill|Katta-mine|ja|刈田嶺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta-mine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Mount Fubo|ja|不忘山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fubo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 蔵王連峰|1=Mount Zaō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q167951}}, it was later named after Zaō Gongen when the deity was enshrined during the Heian period.
In the Edo period, it became a popular pilgrimage site. During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement, shrines like {{ill|Katta Mine Shrine|ja|刈田嶺神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta Mine Shrine|qq=|1=Katta Mine Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396253}} redefined their enshrined deities, but the worship of Zaō Gongen persisted.
== Major Zaō Gongen Sites == * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] (Nara Prefecture) – Important Cultural Property * {{ill|Nyoi-in Temple|ja|如意輪寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoi-in Temple|qq=|1=Nyoirin-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213767}} (Nara Prefecture) – Statue by the Kei school (1226 CE) * [[:en:Mount Ishizuchi|Ishizuchi-san]] temples (Ehime Prefecture) * {{ill|Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|ja|大井蔵王権現神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|qq=|1=Ōi-zaō Gongen Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q43594517}} (Tokyo)

{{Infobox deity|image=File:Zao-Gongen-Kimpusen-Temple-Yoshino.jpg|venerated_in={{ill|Shugendō|qid=Q1483875}} |associated_deities ={{ill|Maitreya|qid=Q193461}}; {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qid=Q11644537}}; {{ill|Thousand-Armed Thousand-Eyed Avalokiteśvara|qid=Q10904910}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q10514059]] Wikidata name: Zaō Gongen [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:蔵王権現立像-Zaō Gongen MET DT5160.jpg|thumb|銅造蔵王権現立像 [[メトロポリタン美術館]]蔵 平安時代（11世紀）<ref>所蔵先館のクレジットラインは以下のとおり。The Harry G. C. Packard Collection of Asian Art, Gift of Harry G. C. Packard, and Purchase, Fletcher, Rogers, Harris Brisbane Dick, and Louis V. Bell Funds, Joseph Pulitzer Bequest, and The Annenberg Fund Inc. Gift, 1975</ref>]] [[File:Zaou Gongen Circumstancial appearance of Mount Yoshino.jpg|thumb|蔵王権現（[[ギメ東洋美術館]]）]] [[File:ŌmineSanjō 2020a.jpg|thumb|湧出岩（[[山上ヶ岳]]）]]
'''Zaō Gongen''' (蔵王権現) is the principal deity of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], a form of Japanese mountain ascetic Buddhism. The formal name is '''Kongō Zaō Gongen''' (金剛蔵王権現) or '''Kongō Zaō Bosatsu''' (金剛蔵王菩薩)<ref>‘Konjaku Monogatari-shū,’ the legend of En no Gyōja.</ref>. This deity is uniquely Japanese, with no origins in Indian Buddhism, and is enshrined as the principal deity of [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The name "Kongō Zaō" signifies the ultimate, indestructible truth and the ruler governing all things<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction’ in ‘The Deity of Shugendō: Zaō Gongen,’ p.10–17.</ref>. The term [[:en:Gongen|Gongen]] means “a deity manifesting temporarily in a provisional form.” Zaō Gongen is believed to encompass the powers of all {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhas|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仏陀|1=Buddha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7055}}, [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattvas]], deities, and spirits<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.18–21, p.26–28.</ref>.
== Origins == Zaō Gongen is said to have manifested to [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]]<ref>A mountain ascetic from the 7th century.</ref> during his training at {{ill|Mount Kinpu|ja|金峰山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kinpu|qq=|1=Mount Kinpusen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10563986}} in Yoshino<ref>Rinetaka Tanaka, ‘Introduction to Shugendō,’ Shueisha Shinsho, 2014, p.54.</ref>. It is considered a composite of {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|qq=|6=ko|7=관세음보살|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, and [[:en:Maitreya|Maitreya]]. At [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] on Mount Yoshino, three nearly identical statues of Zaō Gongen are enshrined together as the temple’s principal icons.
Under the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] system, Zaō Gongen was identified with [[:en:Emperor Ankan|Emperor Ankan]] (Hirokuni Oshitaka Kanehi-no-Mikoto), and during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]] policy, many shrines that previously worshipped Zaō Gongen reidentified their deities as Emperor Ankan.
In [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]], Zaō Gongen became syncretized with deities such as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kunitokotachi]], [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]], and {{ill|Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayamahiko|ja|金山彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanayamahiko|qq=}}. As a result, shrines enshrining Zaō Gongen often list these deities as their enshrined gods.
Zaō Gongen was also equated with [[:en:Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] at [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] in the medieval period, particularly linked through the [[:en:Gakuen-ji|Gakuen-ji]] temple.
== Iconography == === Appearance === Zaō Gongen is depicted similarly to [[:en:Wisdom King|Vidyaraja]], with a fierce, wrathful expression and wild, upward-flowing hair. He usually holds a {{ill|vajra|ja|三鈷杵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/vajra|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金剛杵|1=vajra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q695709}} in his right hand and forms a sword mudra with his left. His right leg is raised high, symbolizing the crushing of evil, while his left foot stands firmly on the ground. Flames blaze behind him, representing wisdom. Some statues, like those at [[:en:Kōryū-ji|Kōryū-ji]] in Kyoto, show both feet grounded, while others have variations with reversed postures.
One notable example is the main deity at {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三佛寺|1=Sanbutsu-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3471689}}’s {{ill|Nageire-dō|ja|投入堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nageire-dō|qq=|1=Nageire-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q19826408}} in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]], a designated Important Cultural Property from the Heian period.
=== Symbolism === The vajra represents the destruction of delusions, while the sword mudra symbolizes cutting through desires. His left foot suppresses demons, and the raised right foot disperses evil between heaven and earth. The flames signify enlightenment<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.22–25.</ref>.
== Zaō Mountain == {{ill|Mount Zaō|ja|蔵王山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Zaō|qq=|1=Mount Zaō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621938}} spans [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]. Originally known as {{ill|Katta-mine|ja|刈田嶺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta-mine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Mount Fubo|ja|不忘山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fubo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 蔵王連峰|1=Mount Zaō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q167951}}, it was later named after Zaō Gongen when the deity was enshrined during the Heian period.
In the Edo period, it became a popular pilgrimage site. During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement, shrines like {{ill|Katta Mine Shrine|ja|刈田嶺神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta Mine Shrine|qq=|1=Katta Mine Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396253}} redefined their enshrined deities, but the worship of Zaō Gongen persisted.
== Major Zaō Gongen Sites == * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] (Nara Prefecture) – Important Cultural Property * {{ill|Nyoi-in Temple|ja|如意輪寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoi-in Temple|qq=|1=Nyoirin-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213767}} (Nara Prefecture) – Statue by the Kei school (1226 CE) * [[:en:Mount Ishizuchi|Ishizuchi-san]] temples (Ehime Prefecture) * {{ill|Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|ja|大井蔵王権現神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|qq=|1=Ōi-zaō Gongen Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q43594517}} (Tokyo)
== Mantras and Seed Syllables == * **Standard Mantra:** "On Bakiryū Sowaka" (オン・バキリュウ・ソワカ)<ref name="mantra">‘The Book of Mudras and Mantras,’ Gakken, 2004, p.152.</ref> * **Other Mantras:** "On Basakusha Aranja Sowaka," "On Basara Kusha Aranja Un Sowaka" * **Seed Syllable:** "हूं" (Hūṃ)<ref name="mantra"/>
== Zaō Gongen in Culture == * {{ill|Arashiyama (Noh)|ja|嵐山 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arashiyama (Noh)|qq=|1=Arashiyama (Noh)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17988500}} – Appears in Noh theatre * {{ill|Kusu (Noh)|ja|国栖 (能)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusu (Noh)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} – Featured in Noh performance * {{ill|Hanagasa Ondo|ja|花笠音頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanagasa Ondo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 花笠まつり|1=hanagasa festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109930449}} – Mentioned in folk songs == See Also == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Buddhas|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の仏尊|1=Japanese Buddhist pantheon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6158131}}

As a result, shrines enshrining Zaō Gongen often list these deities as their enshrined gods.
Zaō Gongen was also equated with [[:en:Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] at [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] in the medieval period, particularly linked through the [[:en:Gakuen-ji|Gakuen-ji]] temple.
== Iconography == === Appearance === Zaō Gongen is depicted similarly to [[:en:Wisdom King|Vidyaraja]], with a fierce, wrathful expression and wild, upward-flowing hair. He usually holds a {{ill|vajra|ja|三鈷杵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/vajra|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金剛杵|1=vajra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q695709}} in his right hand and forms a sword mudra with his left. His right leg is raised high, symbolizing the crushing of evil, while his left foot stands firmly on the ground. Flames blaze behind him, representing wisdom. Some statues, like those at [[:en:Kōryū-ji|Kōryū-ji]] in Kyoto, show both feet grounded, while others have variations with reversed postures.
One notable example is the main deity at {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三佛寺|1=Sanbutsu-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3471689}}’s {{ill|Nageire-dō|ja|投入堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nageire-dō|qq=|1=Nageire-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q19826408}} in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]], a designated Important Cultural Property from the Heian period.
=== Symbolism === The vajra represents the destruction of delusions, while the sword mudra symbolizes cutting through desires. His left foot suppresses demons, and the raised right foot disperses evil between heaven and earth. The flames signify enlightenment<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.22–25.</ref>.
== Zaō Mountain == {{ill|Mount Zaō|ja|蔵王山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Zaō|qq=|1=Mount Zaō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621938}} spans [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]. Originally known as {{ill|Katta-mine|ja|刈田嶺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta-mine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Mount Fubo|ja|不忘山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fubo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 蔵王連峰|1=Mount Zaō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q167951}}, it was later named after Zaō Gongen when the deity was enshrined during the Heian period.
In the Edo period, it became a popular pilgrimage site. During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement, shrines like {{ill|Katta Mine Shrine|ja|刈田嶺神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta Mine Shrine|qq=|1=Katta Mine Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396253}} redefined their enshrined deities, but the worship of Zaō Gongen persisted.
== Major Zaō Gongen Sites == * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] (Nara Prefecture) – Important Cultural Property * {{ill|Nyoi-in Temple|ja|如意輪寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoi-in Temple|qq=|1=Nyoirin-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213767}} (Nara Prefecture) – Statue by the Kei school (1226 CE) * [[:en:Mount Ishizuchi|Ishizuchi-san]] temples (Ehime Prefecture) * {{ill|Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|ja|大井蔵王権現神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|qq=|1=Ōi-zaō Gongen Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q43594517}} (Tokyo)

Gift, 1975</ref>]] [[File:Zaou Gongen Circumstancial appearance of Mount Yoshino.jpg|thumb|蔵王権現（[[ギメ東洋美術館]]）]] [[File:ŌmineSanjō 2020a.jpg|thumb|湧出岩（[[山上ヶ岳]]）]]
'''Zaō Gongen''' (蔵王権現) is the principal deity of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendō]], a form of Japanese mountain ascetic Buddhism. The formal name is '''Kongō Zaō Gongen''' (金剛蔵王権現) or '''Kongō Zaō Bosatsu''' (金剛蔵王菩薩)<ref>‘Konjaku Monogatari-shū,’ the legend of En no Gyōja.</ref>. This deity is uniquely Japanese, with no origins in Indian Buddhism, and is enshrined as the principal deity of [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] in [[:en:Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The name "Kongō Zaō" signifies the ultimate, indestructible truth and the ruler governing all things<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction’ in ‘The Deity of Shugendō: Zaō Gongen,’ p.10–17.</ref>. The term [[:en:Gongen|Gongen]] means “a deity manifesting temporarily in a provisional form.” Zaō Gongen is believed to encompass the powers of all {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhas|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仏陀|1=Buddha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7055}}, [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattvas]], deities, and spirits<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.18–21, p.26–28.</ref>.
== Origins == Zaō Gongen is said to have manifested to [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]]<ref>A mountain ascetic from the 7th century.</ref> during his training at {{ill|Mount Kinpu|ja|金峰山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kinpu|qq=|1=Mount Kinpusen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10563986}} in Yoshino<ref>Rinetaka Tanaka, ‘Introduction to Shugendō,’ Shueisha Shinsho, 2014, p.54.</ref>. It is considered a composite of {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|qq=|6=ko|7=관세음보살|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, and [[:en:Maitreya|Maitreya]]. At [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] on Mount Yoshino, three nearly identical statues of Zaō Gongen are enshrined together as the temple’s principal icons.
Under the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] system, Zaō Gongen was identified with [[:en:Emperor Ankan|Emperor Ankan]] (Hirokuni Oshitaka Kanehi-no-Mikoto), and during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]] policy, many shrines that previously worshipped Zaō Gongen reidentified their deities as Emperor Ankan.
In [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]], Zaō Gongen became syncretized with deities such as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kunitokotachi]], [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]], and {{ill|Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayamahiko|ja|金山彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanayamahiko|qq=}}. As a result, shrines enshrining Zaō Gongen often list these deities as their enshrined gods.
Zaō Gongen was also equated with [[:en:Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] at [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] in the medieval period, particularly linked through the [[:en:Gakuen-ji|Gakuen-ji]] temple.
== Iconography == === Appearance === Zaō Gongen is depicted similarly to [[:en:Wisdom King|Vidyaraja]], with a fierce, wrathful expression and wild, upward-flowing hair. He usually holds a {{ill|vajra|ja|三鈷杵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/vajra|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金剛杵|1=vajra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q695709}} in his right hand and forms a sword mudra with his left. His right leg is raised high, symbolizing the crushing of evil, while his left foot stands firmly on the ground. Flames blaze behind him, representing wisdom. Some statues, like those at [[:en:Kōryū-ji|Kōryū-ji]] in Kyoto, show both feet grounded, while others have variations with reversed postures.
One notable example is the main deity at {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三佛寺|1=Sanbutsu-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3471689}}’s {{ill|Nageire-dō|ja|投入堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nageire-dō|qq=|1=Nageire-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q19826408}} in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]], a designated Important Cultural Property from the Heian period.
=== Symbolism === The vajra represents the destruction of delusions, while the sword mudra symbolizes cutting through desires. His left foot suppresses demons, and the raised right foot disperses evil between heaven and earth. The flames signify enlightenment<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.22–25.</ref>.
== Zaō Mountain == {{ill|Mount Zaō|ja|蔵王山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Zaō|qq=|1=Mount Zaō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621938}} spans [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]. Originally known as {{ill|Katta-mine|ja|刈田嶺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta-mine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Mount Fubo|ja|不忘山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fubo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 蔵王連峰|1=Mount Zaō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q167951}}, it was later named after Zaō Gongen when the deity was enshrined during the Heian period.
In the Edo period, it became a popular pilgrimage site. During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement, shrines like {{ill|Katta Mine Shrine|ja|刈田嶺神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta Mine Shrine|qq=|1=Katta Mine Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396253}} redefined their enshrined deities, but the worship of Zaō Gongen persisted.
== Major Zaō Gongen Sites == * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] (Nara Prefecture) – Important Cultural Property * {{ill|Nyoi-in Temple|ja|如意輪寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoi-in Temple|qq=|1=Nyoirin-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213767}} (Nara Prefecture) – Statue by the Kei school (1226 CE) * [[:en:Mount Ishizuchi|Ishizuchi-san]] temples (Ehime Prefecture) * {{ill|Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|ja|大井蔵王権現神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|qq=|1=Ōi-zaō Gongen Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q43594517}} (Tokyo)
== Mantras and Seed Syllables == * **Standard Mantra:** "On Bakiryū Sowaka" (オン・バキリュウ・ソワカ)<ref name="mantra">‘The Book of Mudras and Mantras,’ Gakken, 2004, p.152.</ref> * **Other Mantras:** "On Basakusha Aranja Sowaka," "On Basara Kusha Aranja Un Sowaka" * **Seed Syllable:** "हूं" (Hūṃ)<ref name="mantra"/>

It is considered a composite of {{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|ja|釈迦如来|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qq=}}, {{ill|Kannon|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|qq=|6=ko|7=관세음보살|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannon|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, and [[:en:Maitreya|Maitreya]]. At [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] on Mount Yoshino, three nearly identical statues of Zaō Gongen are enshrined together as the temple’s principal icons.
Under the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] system, Zaō Gongen was identified with [[:en:Emperor Ankan|Emperor Ankan]] (Hirokuni Oshitaka Kanehi-no-Mikoto), and during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]] policy, many shrines that previously worshipped Zaō Gongen reidentified their deities as Emperor Ankan.
In [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]], Zaō Gongen became syncretized with deities such as {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kunitokotachi]], [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]], and {{ill|Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayamahiko|ja|金山彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanayamahiko|qq=}}. As a result, shrines enshrining Zaō Gongen often list these deities as their enshrined gods.
Zaō Gongen was also equated with [[:en:Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] at [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] in the medieval period, particularly linked through the [[:en:Gakuen-ji|Gakuen-ji]] temple.
== Iconography == === Appearance === Zaō Gongen is depicted similarly to [[:en:Wisdom King|Vidyaraja]], with a fierce, wrathful expression and wild, upward-flowing hair. He usually holds a {{ill|vajra|ja|三鈷杵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/vajra|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金剛杵|1=vajra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q695709}} in his right hand and forms a sword mudra with his left. His right leg is raised high, symbolizing the crushing of evil, while his left foot stands firmly on the ground. Flames blaze behind him, representing wisdom. Some statues, like those at [[:en:Kōryū-ji|Kōryū-ji]] in Kyoto, show both feet grounded, while others have variations with reversed postures.
One notable example is the main deity at {{ill|Sanbutsu-ji|ja|三仏寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanbutsu-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三佛寺|1=Sanbutsu-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3471689}}’s {{ill|Nageire-dō|ja|投入堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nageire-dō|qq=|1=Nageire-dō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q19826408}} in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]], a designated Important Cultural Property from the Heian period.
=== Symbolism === The vajra represents the destruction of delusions, while the sword mudra symbolizes cutting through desires. His left foot suppresses demons, and the raised right foot disperses evil between heaven and earth. The flames signify enlightenment<ref>‘Zaō Gongen Introduction,’ p.22–25.</ref>.
== Zaō Mountain == {{ill|Mount Zaō|ja|蔵王山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Zaō|qq=|1=Mount Zaō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621938}} spans [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]] and [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]]. Originally known as {{ill|Katta-mine|ja|刈田嶺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta-mine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Mount Fubo|ja|不忘山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Fubo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 蔵王連峰|1=Mount Zaō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q167951}}, it was later named after Zaō Gongen when the deity was enshrined during the Heian period.
In the Edo period, it became a popular pilgrimage site. During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] movement, shrines like {{ill|Katta Mine Shrine|ja|刈田嶺神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katta Mine Shrine|qq=|1=Katta Mine Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396253}} redefined their enshrined deities, but the worship of Zaō Gongen persisted.
== Major Zaō Gongen Sites == * [[:en:Kimpusen-ji|Kinpusen-ji]] (Nara Prefecture) – Important Cultural Property * {{ill|Nyoi-in Temple|ja|如意輪寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoi-in Temple|qq=|1=Nyoirin-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213767}} (Nara Prefecture) – Statue by the Kei school (1226 CE) * [[:en:Mount Ishizuchi|Ishizuchi-san]] temples (Ehime Prefecture) * {{ill|Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|ja|大井蔵王権現神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Zaō Gongen Shrine|qq=|1=Ōi-zaō Gongen Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q43594517}} (Tokyo)
== Mantras and Seed Syllables == * **Standard Mantra:** "On Bakiryū Sowaka" (オン・バキリュウ・ソワカ)<ref name="mantra">‘The Book of Mudras and Mantras,’ Gakken, 2004, p.152.</ref> * **Other Mantras:** "On Basakusha Aranja Sowaka," "On Basara Kusha Aranja Un Sowaka" * **Seed Syllable:** "हूं" (Hūṃ)<ref name="mantra"/>
